cheatSheet:
# Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Given a long URL, generate a short URL (e.g., tinyurl.com/abc123)
- Redirect short URL to original long URL
- Custom aliases (optional): users can pick their own short code
- Expiration: links can expire after a set time
- Analytics: click counts, referrer, geography

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability (99.99% uptime)
- Low latency redirects (<10ms p99)
- Short URLs must be unique and not predictable
- Read-heavy: ~100:1 read/write ratio

## 2. Capacity Estimation

### Traffic
- Write: 100M new URLs/day = ~1,200 writes/sec
- Read: 10B redirects/day = ~116,000 reads/sec
- Peak: 3x average = ~350,000 reads/sec

### Storage
- Each URL record: ~500 bytes (long URL up to 2KB avg ~200B + metadata)
- 100M URLs/day x 365 x 5 years = 182.5B records... unrealistic
- Assume 100M total URLs stored: 100M x 500B = 50GB (very manageable)
- With analytics events: 10B events/day x 100B = 1TB/day (needs separate store)

### Bandwidth
- Write: 1,200 req/s x 500B = 600 KB/s
- Read: 116,000 req/s x 100B response = ~12 MB/s

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Client Browser/App])
    LB[Load Balancer]
    WS[Write Service]
    RS[Read Service / Redirect Service]
    Cache[Redis Cache Cluster]
    DB[(Primary SQL DB)]
    RDB[(Read Replicas)]
    MQ[Message Queue Kafka]
    AS[Analytics Service]
    ADB[(Analytics DB - Cassandra)]
    CDN[CDN Edge Nodes]

    Client -->|POST /shorten| LB
    Client -->|GET /abc123| CDN
    CDN -->|Cache miss| LB
    LB --> WS
    LB --> RS
    WS --> DB
    WS --> Cache
    RS --> Cache
    Cache -->|Miss| RDB
    RS -->|Click event| MQ
    MQ --> AS
    AS --> ADB
```

## 4. URL Shortening Algorithm

### Option A: Base62 Encoding of Auto-Increment ID
- DB auto-increments ID (e.g., 123456789)
- Encode to Base62: `[0-9a-zA-Z]` = 62 chars
- 6 chars = 62^6 = ~56 billion unique URLs
- 7 chars = 62^7 = ~3.5 trillion
- **Problem**: predictable, sequential IDs expose business metrics

### Option B: MD5/SHA256 Hash + Truncate
- Hash the long URL: `MD5(longURL)` → 128-bit hex
- Take first 6 chars of Base62 encoding
- **Collision probability**: with 100M URLs, ~0.001% — need collision check
- **Problem**: two users shortening same URL get same short code (can be a feature)

### Option C: Snowflake-style ID + Base62 (Recommended)
- Generate 64-bit ID using distributed ID generator (Twitter Snowflake)
- Components: timestamp (41 bits) + datacenter (5 bits) + machine (5 bits) + sequence (12 bits)
- Encode ID to Base62 → ~7-char short code
- No collision possible, globally unique, time-ordered

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant WS as Write Service
    participant ID as ID Generator
    participant DB as Database
    participant Cache as Redis

    C->>WS: POST /shorten {url, alias?, ttl?}
    WS->>WS: Validate URL format
    alt Custom alias requested
        WS->>DB: Check alias availability
    else No alias
        WS->>ID: Generate Snowflake ID
        ID-->>WS: 64-bit ID
        WS->>WS: Base62 encode → short_code
    end
    WS->>DB: INSERT url_mapping
    WS->>Cache: SET short_code → long_url TTL
    WS-->>C: 201 {short_url: tinyurl.com/abc123}
```

## 5. Data Model

### URL Mappings Table (PostgreSQL)
```sql
CREATE TABLE url_mappings (
    short_code   VARCHAR(10)  PRIMARY KEY,
    long_url     TEXT         NOT NULL,
    user_id      BIGINT,
    created_at   TIMESTAMP    DEFAULT NOW(),
    expires_at   TIMESTAMP,
    is_active    BOOLEAN      DEFAULT TRUE,
    custom_alias BOOLEAN      DEFAULT FALSE
);
CREATE INDEX idx_user_urls ON url_mappings(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_expires ON url_mappings(expires_at) WHERE expires_at IS NOT NULL;
```

### Analytics Events (Cassandra)
```
CREATE TABLE click_events (
    short_code   TEXT,
    clicked_at   TIMESTAMP,
    ip_address   TEXT,
    user_agent   TEXT,
    referrer     TEXT,
    country      TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (short_code, clicked_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (clicked_at DESC);
```

## 6. API Design

```
POST /api/v1/shorten
Body: { "url": "https://...", "alias": "mylink", "ttl_days": 30 }
Response 201: { "short_url": "https://tinyurl.com/mylink", "expires_at": "..." }

GET /{short_code}
Response 301/302: Location: https://original-long-url.com
(Use 301 for permanent, 302 for trackable redirects)

GET /api/v1/analytics/{short_code}
Query: ?from=2024-01-01&to=2024-01-31
Response 200: { "total_clicks": 15234, "by_country": {...}, "by_day": [...] }

DELETE /api/v1/urls/{short_code}
Response 204

PUT /api/v1/urls/{short_code}
Body: { "expires_at": "...", "is_active": false }
Response 200
```

## 7. Redirect Strategy: 301 vs 302
- **301 Permanent**: Browser caches redirect. Reduces server load. Analytics misses repeat visits from same browser.
- **302 Temporary**: Every redirect goes through server. Accurate analytics. Higher latency.
- **Recommendation**: Use 302 for analytics-enabled links; 301 for "dumb" links.

## 8. Caching Strategy
- Cache top 20% URLs (80% of traffic via Pareto principle)
- Redis with LRU eviction
- TTL in cache aligned with link expiration
- Cache key: `url:{short_code}` → long_url
- Cache-aside pattern: read from cache first, fallback to DB
- Write-through on creation

## 9. Scalability & Bottlenecks

### Database Scaling
- Start with single PostgreSQL + read replicas
- Shard by short_code prefix when write load exceeds single node
- Consider DynamoDB for pure key-value access pattern (short_code → long_url)

### ID Generation at Scale
- Deploy multiple Snowflake ID generator instances (stateless)
- Each instance has unique machine ID assigned at startup via ZooKeeper

### Expiration Handling
- Background job (cron) to soft-delete expired URLs
- Redis TTL auto-evicts cached expired links
- For hard deletes: batch job during off-peak hours

### Custom Alias Conflicts
- Optimistic locking: attempt INSERT, catch unique constraint violation
- Return 409 Conflict to client

## 10. Monitoring
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 for redirect endpoint
- Cache hit rate (target: >95%)
- Error rate (4xx: invalid codes, 5xx: service errors)
- DB connection pool utilization
- Queue lag for analytics pipeline
- Alerts: redirect latency >50ms, cache hit rate <90%

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows what a URL shortener does. Can describe the basic redirect flow. Understands hash functions at a surface level.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Can design single-server solution with a database. Understands 301 vs 302. Can implement Base62 encoding.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs scalable multi-server architecture. Handles cache layer, read replicas, analytics pipeline. Discusses collision handling.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Handles global distribution, custom aliases with race conditions, sharding strategy, SLA guarantees, and detailed capacity planning.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs for planet-scale (billions of URLs), multi-region active-active, discusses consistency models, zero-downtime migrations, and cost optimization.

plan.overview: 10-session plan to master URL shortener design from basics to expert-level trade-off discussions.

sessions.title: Problem Scoping & Requirements
sessions.goals: Practice asking clarifying questions. Define functional vs non-functional requirements. Identify the read/write ratio.
activities: Role-play: interviewer gives 'Design TinyURL'. Practice asking 10 clarifying questions.
Write out functional and non-functional requirements.
Identify that this is a read-heavy system (100:1 ratio).

sessions.title: Capacity Estimation
sessions.goals: Build fluency in back-of-envelope math. Estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth.
activities: Estimate storage for 5 years of URL data.
Estimate cache size needed for top 20% URLs.

sessions.title: URL Shortening Algorithms
sessions.goals: Understand and compare all shortening approaches. Implement Base62.
activities: Compare MD5 hash approach vs auto-increment vs Snowflake.
Analyze collision probability for hash approach.
Decide when each approach is appropriate.

sessions.title: High-Level Architecture
sessions.goals: Draw the full system diagram. Identify all components.
activities: Draw architecture: LB, write service, read service, DB, cache, MQ, analytics.
Draw the request flow for create and redirect.

sessions.title: Database Design
sessions.goals: Design the data model. Choose between SQL and NoSQL.
activities: Design url_mappings table with all fields.
Design analytics events schema in Cassandra.
Discuss SQL vs DynamoDB for the core mapping store.

sessions.title: Caching Strategy
sessions.goals: Deep dive into caching layer design.
activities: Handle cache invalidation on URL deletion/expiration.

sessions.title: Scalability Deep Dive
sessions.goals: Handle scale bottlenecks. Shard the database.
activities: Identify the write bottleneck at scale.
Add CDN layer for geographic distribution.

sessions.title: API Design & Edge Cases
sessions.goals: Design RESTful API. Handle edge cases.
activities: Design all API endpoints with request/response.

sessions.title: Analytics Pipeline
activities: Design Kafka event pipeline for click events.
Choose between real-time (Flink) and batch (Spark) analytics.
Design Cassandra schema for time-series click data.

sessions.title: Mock Interview
sessions.goals: Full mock interview from scratch in 45 minutes.
activities: Start from blank slate. No notes.
Spend 5 min on requirements, 5 min on estimation, 20 min on architecture, 10 min on deep dive, 5 min on trade-offs.

quizBank.question: What HTTP status code should a URL shortener return for a permanent redirect?
quizBank.answer: 301 Moved Permanently
quizBank.explanation: 301 tells browsers to cache the redirect permanently. However, 302 is often preferred for trackable links since it forces every request through the server.

quizBank.question: With Base62 encoding using 6 characters, how many unique short URLs can be generated?
quizBank.answer: ~56 billion
quizBank.explanation: 62^6 = 56,800,235,584 ≈ 56 billion. For 100M URLs/day this gives 568 days of capacity, so 7 characters (62^7 ≈ 3.5T) is safer for long-lived systems.

quizBank.question: Which ID generation strategy avoids collisions entirely without database lookups?
quizBank.answer: Twitter Snowflake IDs
quizBank.explanation: Snowflake IDs are composed of timestamp + datacenter + machine + sequence bits, guaranteeing global uniqueness without coordination or collision checks.

quizBank.question: A URL shortener has 100M URLs and uses MD5 hash truncated to 6 Base62 chars. What is the main risk?
quizBank.answer: Hash collisions requiring retry logic
quizBank.explanation: With 56B possible values and 100M URLs stored, birthday paradox gives ~0.1% collision rate. The system must detect collisions and retry with a different hash (e.g., append salt).

quizBank.question: Two users simultaneously request a custom alias 'mylink'. How do you handle this safely?
options: Use database unique constraint + catch exception
Both B and C are valid approaches
quizBank.answer: Both B and C are valid approaches
quizBank.explanation: DB unique constraint provides atomicity at the persistence layer. Redis SETNX (SET if Not eXists) provides a distributed lock. Using both gives defense in depth. Catch the constraint violation and return 409 Conflict.

quizBank.question: Your URL shortener's Redis cache suddenly goes down. What happens to the system?
quizBank.answer: All reads fall through to the database. At 116,000 reads/sec, the database (typically handles ~10,000-50,000 reads/sec) will be overwhelmed and likely crash, causing a cascade failure. Mitigation: circuit breaker pattern to shed load, read replicas, graceful degradation. Prevention: Redis Cluster or Sentinel for HA.

quizBank.question: Your URL shortener goes viral — a single short URL is clicked 1 million times per second. How do you handle this 'hot key' problem?
quizBank.answer: The Redis key for that URL becomes a hot key. Solutions: (1) Local in-process cache (LRU) in each app server instance — reduces Redis calls by 100x. (2) Redis client-side caching. (3) Replicate hot keys across multiple Redis nodes. (4) CDN-level caching for the redirect response — the CDN absorbs all traffic for truly viral links.

quizBank.question: Why would you use Cassandra for analytics instead of PostgreSQL?
quizBank.answer: Cassandra excels at time-series write-heavy workloads. Click events are append-only, partitioned by short_code, clustered by time. Cassandra's wide-row model handles billions of events with linear scalability. PostgreSQL would require aggressive partitioning and struggle at 10B events/day write rate. Cassandra trades strong consistency for write throughput and availability.

quizBank.question: What caching eviction policy is most appropriate for URL shortener cache?
quizBank.answer: LRU (Least Recently Used)
quizBank.explanation: LRU evicts URLs not accessed recently. This matches traffic patterns — recently used links are likely to be used again. LFU could also work but requires frequency counters which add memory overhead.

quizBank.question: You need to add a feature: 'show all URLs created by user X'. How does this affect your database design?
quizBank.answer: Add user_id index to url_mappings table. With sharding by short_code, user's URLs are scattered across shards — a scatter-gather query is expensive. Better: maintain a secondary index table (user_id → [short_codes]) or use a NoSQL store like DynamoDB with a GSI on user_id. Or shard by user_id for user-centric queries, but then redirect lookup (by short_code) requires routing logic.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a 'tombstone' record when a short URL is deleted?
options: To mark record as deleted while keeping the short code reserved
To notify CDN to purge cache
quizBank.answer: To mark record as deleted while keeping the short code reserved
quizBank.explanation: Soft delete with is_active=false prevents the short code from being reassigned (which could create confusion if old links are bookmarked). Hard deletes risk code reuse. The tombstone approach is safer.

quizBank.question: How would you design a multi-region active-active URL shortener with no single region as master?
quizBank.answer: Use CRDTs or Last-Write-Wins with vector clocks for conflict resolution. Snowflake IDs encode datacenter ID to prevent cross-region ID collisions. Each region writes to local DB; async replication cross-region. For redirects, route to nearest region — they all have the URL mapping (eventual consistency). Custom aliases could conflict: use a global coordination layer (like Google Spanner) or use region prefix in aliases. Accept that new URLs may not be available in all regions for ~100ms.

quizBank.question: What is the read/write ratio typically assumed for a URL shortener?
quizBank.explanation: URL shorteners are extremely read-heavy. Once created, a short URL may be clicked thousands of times. The 100:1 ratio drives architecture decisions: aggressive caching, read replicas, CDN.

quizBank.question: A customer complains that clicking a link shows 'URL not found' even though the link was created 1 hour ago. What could cause this?
quizBank.answer: Possible causes: (1) Created on primary DB but read hits a read replica with replication lag. (2) Cache was populated with 'not found' response (negative caching) and hasn't expired. (3) URL was created but transaction rolled back (partial failure). (4) Link expired (TTL set incorrectly). Debugging: check DB replication lag, check cache for negative entries, check creation logs.

quizBank.question: Should you use 301 or 302 redirect for maximum analytics accuracy? What are the trade-offs?
quizBank.answer: Use 302 (temporary) for analytics: every click routes through your server, enabling accurate counting. 301 (permanent) gets cached by browsers — repeat clicks never hit your server, so analytics undercounts. Trade-off: 302 adds latency (one extra network hop) and server load. For high-traffic links, this can be significant. Some services use 301 with no caching headers (Cache-Control: no-store) to get best of both, but this increases server load.

quizBank.question: Your URL shortener needs to handle 350,000 redirect requests/sec at peak. How many Redis nodes do you need, assuming each handles 100,000 ops/sec?
quizBank.answer: 4 nodes
quizBank.explanation: 350,000 / 100,000 = 3.5, round up to 4 nodes. Add buffer for failures (N+1): 5 nodes in production. Use Redis Cluster for automatic sharding and failover.

quizBank.question: How would you implement link expiration at scale without scanning the entire database?
quizBank.answer: Multiple approaches: (1) Redis TTL: set cache entry TTL = link TTL. Cache auto-expires. (2) DB index on expires_at: background cron job queries WHERE expires_at < NOW() AND is_active = true, processes in batches. (3) Event-driven: at creation time, publish a 'expire at T' event to a delay queue (e.g., SQS delay or scheduled Kafka). (4) Lazy expiration: check expires_at on every read, return 410 Gone if expired. Combination of lazy + background cleanup is most practical.

quizBank.question: How would you prevent your URL shortener from being used to spread malicious links?
quizBank.answer: Multi-layer approach: (1) Google Safe Browsing API: check URL before shortening. (2) URL validation: reject malformed URLs, known bad domains (blocklist). (3) Rate limiting: limit URLs created per IP/user per hour. (4) CAPTCHA for anonymous users. (5) Async scan: allow creation but scan async, deactivate if malicious. (6) Report abuse endpoint: user-reported links reviewed and deactivated. (7) Honeypot detection for bots. (8) For clicks: redirect through interstitial warning page for unverified links.

quizBank.question: Which data store is most appropriate for storing the core short_code → long_url mapping at scale?
options: DynamoDB with short_code as partition key
quizBank.answer: DynamoDB with short_code as partition key
quizBank.explanation: The access pattern is pure key-value: given short_code, return long_url. DynamoDB's single-digit millisecond latency, serverless scaling, and built-in TTL make it ideal. MySQL requires careful sharding. Elasticsearch is overkill. MongoDB adds unnecessary complexity.

quizBank.question: How would you migrate from auto-increment IDs to Snowflake IDs in a live production system with zero downtime?
quizBank.answer: Dual-write migration: (1) Add new snowflake_id column (nullable) alongside existing id. (2) Deploy new ID generator service. (3) Update write path: generate both old ID and Snowflake ID, store both. (4) Backfill: background job generates Snowflake IDs for existing rows. (5) Update read path: try Snowflake lookup first, fallback to old ID lookup. (6) Once backfill complete and old ID traffic = 0, drop old column. (7) Total migration: weeks to months depending on data volume. Key: never a hard cutover, always a gradual rollout with feature flags.

quizBank.question: What happens when the same long URL is shortened twice by two different users?
quizBank.answer: Two behaviors are possible: (1) Idempotent: same long URL always maps to same short code (deduplicate). Saves storage but reveals to user B that user A also shortened the URL (privacy leak). (2) Non-idempotent: create two separate short codes for the same long URL. More private, uses more storage, more flexible (different TTLs, analytics per user). Most services (TinyURL, Bitly) use non-idempotent approach for privacy.

quizBank.question: You want to guarantee that a short URL redirect returns in <10ms p99. Which architecture achieves this?
quizBank.answer: Redis cache + CDN edge caching
quizBank.explanation: CDN edge nodes cache 301/302 responses geographically close to users. Cache hit at CDN edge = 1-5ms. Redis cache handles CDN misses at 1-2ms. This two-tier caching achieves <10ms p99 globally. DynamoDB DAX is also valid but adds cost and complexity.

cheatSheet:
# Design a Chat System (WhatsApp/Slack)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- One-on-one messaging
- Group messaging (up to 500 members)
- Online/offline presence indicators
- Message delivery receipts (sent, delivered, read)
- Media sharing (images, videos, files)
- End-to-end encryption (optional scope)
- Message history / persistence
- Push notifications for offline users

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Low latency: messages delivered in <100ms p99
- High availability: 99.99% uptime
- Consistency: messages must appear in correct order
- Scale: 500M daily active users, 100B messages/day

## 2. Capacity Estimation

### Traffic
- 500M DAU, each sends ~20 messages/day
- Write: 500M x 20 / 86,400 = ~116,000 messages/sec
- Read: assume 10x read multiplier = ~1.16M reads/sec (group messages amplify)
- Peak: 3x = ~350,000 writes/sec

### Storage
- Message size: ~200 bytes average (text)
- 100B messages/day x 200B = 20TB/day
- Media: assume 10% of messages have media, avg 500KB = 5PB/day (CDN/object store)
- Text storage for 5 years: 20TB x 365 x 5 = 36.5PB (need tiered storage)

### Connections
- 500M DAU, each maintains 1 WebSocket connection
- Assume 20% concurrent at peak = 100M simultaneous connections
- Connection servers: each handles ~50,000 connections → 2,000 servers

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    App([Mobile/Web App])
    LB[Load Balancer / API Gateway]
    CS[Chat Server WebSocket]
    PS[Presence Service]
    NS[Notification Service]
    MS[Message Store Service]
    GS[Group Service]
    MQ[Message Queue Kafka]
    Cache[Redis Pub/Sub + Session Cache]
    MDB[(Message DB - Cassandra)]
    UDB[(User/Group DB - PostgreSQL)]
    OS[Object Store S3]
    CDN[CDN]
    FCM[FCM / APNS]

    App <-->|WebSocket| LB
    LB <--> CS
    CS <-->|Pub/Sub| Cache
    CS --> MQ
    MQ --> MS
    MQ --> NS
    MQ --> PS
    MS --> MDB
    GS --> UDB
    NS --> FCM
    CS --> OS
    OS --> CDN
    App -->|Media| CDN
```

## 4. Real-Time Messaging: WebSocket Deep Dive

### Why WebSocket over HTTP polling?
- HTTP long-polling: client polls server repeatedly. High latency (up to poll interval), high server overhead.
- WebSocket: persistent bidirectional connection. Sub-10ms latency, efficient for chat.
- Server-Sent Events (SSE): one-way server→client. Good for notifications but not chat.

### Connection Management
- Each user connects to a Chat Server (CS)
- CS registers user → server mapping in Redis: `user:{user_id}:server` = `cs-server-42`
- When message arrives for user, look up their server, route via internal message bus

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice App
    participant CS1 as Chat Server 1
    participant Redis as Redis Pub/Sub
    participant CS2 as Chat Server 2
    participant B as Bob App
    participant MQ as Kafka
    participant DB as Cassandra

    A->>CS1: WebSocket: {to: Bob, msg: "Hi"}
    CS1->>MQ: Publish message event
    CS1->>Redis: PUBLISH channel:bob "message payload"
    Redis->>CS2: Message for Bob (Bob connected to CS2)
    CS2->>B: WebSocket: deliver message
    MQ->>DB: Persist message asynchronously
    B->>CS2: ACK delivered
    CS2->>CS1: Delivery receipt
    CS1->>A: Message delivered ✓
```

## 5. Message Delivery Guarantees

### At-Least-Once Delivery
- Message published to Kafka (durable log)
- Consumer (Message Store) persists to Cassandra
- If consumer crashes, Kafka replays from last committed offset

### Exactly-Once Semantics
- Each message has a unique `message_id` (Snowflake ID)
- DB write is idempotent: `INSERT IF NOT EXISTS` by message_id
- Prevents duplicate messages on retry

### Message Ordering
- Per-conversation ordering via Cassandra clustering key on timestamp
- Lamport clocks or hybrid logical clocks for distributed ordering
- Client-side sequence numbers for UI ordering

## 6. Data Model

### Messages Table (Cassandra)
```sql
CREATE TABLE messages (
    conversation_id  UUID,
    message_id       BIGINT,   -- Snowflake (time-ordered)
    sender_id        BIGINT,
    content          TEXT,
    media_url        TEXT,
    message_type     TEXT,     -- text, image, video, file
    status           TEXT,     -- sent, delivered, read
    created_at       TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC);
```

### Conversations Table (PostgreSQL)
```sql
CREATE TABLE conversations (
    id           UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    type         VARCHAR(10),  -- direct, group
    name         VARCHAR(255), -- for group
    created_at   TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at   TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE conversation_members (
    conversation_id  UUID,
    user_id          BIGINT,
    role             VARCHAR(20),  -- member, admin
    last_read_id     BIGINT,       -- for unread count
    joined_at        TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, user_id)
);
```

## 7. Presence System

### Design
- User sends heartbeat every 5 seconds while active
- Presence server updates Redis: `presence:{user_id}` = timestamp TTL 10s
- Friend's clients subscribe to presence updates via WebSocket

### Scaling Presence
- 500M users, each has avg 200 friends
- Presence change → fan-out to 200 friends
- Problem: 100M presence events/sec at peak
- Solution: push presence only for users in active conversations, batch presence updates

## 8. API Design

```
WebSocket Connection:
wss://chat.example.com/ws?token=JWT

Message Events (JSON over WebSocket):
{ "type": "message", "to": "conv_id", "content": "Hello", "message_id": "client_gen_id" }
{ "type": "ack", "message_id": "...", "status": "delivered" }
{ "type": "typing", "conversation_id": "...", "is_typing": true }
{ "type": "presence", "user_id": "...", "status": "online" }

REST APIs:
POST /api/v1/conversations           — create conversation
GET  /api/v1/conversations           — list user's conversations
GET  /api/v1/conversations/{id}/messages?before={msg_id}&limit=50  — paginate history
POST /api/v1/conversations/{id}/media  — upload media, get presigned URL
PUT  /api/v1/messages/{id}/read      — mark as read
```

## 9. Group Chat Architecture

### Fan-out on Write
- When a message sent to group of 500 members:
- Write message to DB once (single record)
- Fan-out: for each online member's chat server, publish via Redis Pub/Sub
- For offline members: push notification via FCM/APNS
- **Problem**: 500-member group with 1M messages/day = 500M fan-out operations/day

### Hybrid Approach
- Small groups (<100): fan-out on write
- Large groups (100+): fan-out on read — members pull messages when they open the chat

## 10. Media Handling
- Client requests presigned S3 upload URL from server
- Client uploads directly to S3 (bypasses chat servers)
- Server receives media_url after upload confirmation
- CDN in front of S3 for download
- Thumbnail generation: Lambda triggered on S3 upload

## 11. Push Notifications (Offline Users)
- Kafka consumer: Notification Service
- Checks if recipient is offline (presence cache miss)
- Sends via FCM (Android) / APNS (iOS) / Web Push
- Fallback: SMS for critical notifications
- Rate limit: batch notifications for users with >10 unread messages

## 12. Scalability & Monitoring
- WebSocket servers: stateful — use consistent hashing for connection routing
- Kafka: partition by conversation_id for ordered processing
- Cassandra: partition by conversation_id — even distribution
- Monitor: message delivery latency p99, WebSocket connection count, Kafka consumer lag, Cassandra write latency

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Understands chat as client-server. Knows HTTP but not WebSocket. Can describe basic message sending.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands WebSocket. Can design single-server chat. Knows about message persistence in a database.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs multi-server chat with routing. Uses Redis Pub/Sub for cross-server delivery. Handles group chat. Designs Cassandra schema.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Handles scale: 100M concurrent connections, presence system optimization, large group fan-out strategies, media upload pipeline.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs end-to-end encryption (Signal Protocol), multi-region chat, message ordering with hybrid logical clocks, exactly-once semantics.

plan.overview: 10-session plan to master chat system design covering real-time protocols, scale, and advanced features.

quizBank.question: Why is WebSocket preferred over HTTP for a chat application?
options: WebSocket is bidirectional and maintains persistent connection
quizBank.answer: WebSocket is bidirectional and maintains persistent connection
quizBank.explanation: HTTP is request-response (client initiates). WebSocket allows server to push messages to client at any time with low overhead. This is essential for real-time chat delivery.

quizBank.question: Alice is connected to Chat Server 1. Bob is connected to Chat Server 2. Alice sends Bob a message. How is it delivered?
options: Alice's server sends directly to Bob's server via HTTP
Message is stored in DB, Bob polls for new messages
Message is published to shared Redis Pub/Sub channel, Bob's server is subscribed
A centralized router server handles all cross-server delivery
quizBank.answer: Message is published to shared Redis Pub/Sub channel, Bob's server is subscribed
quizBank.explanation: Redis Pub/Sub is a common pattern for cross-server message routing. Each chat server subscribes to channels for the users connected to it. When a message arrives for Bob, it's published to his channel, and his server delivers it via WebSocket.

quizBank.question: Why is Cassandra chosen for message storage over PostgreSQL?
options: Cassandra handles write-heavy time-series workloads with linear scalability
quizBank.answer: Cassandra handles write-heavy time-series workloads with linear scalability
quizBank.explanation: Messages are append-only, partitioned by conversation_id, clustered by time. Cassandra's LSM-tree storage engine handles high write throughput. PostgreSQL would require complex sharding to match the write scale of 100B messages/day.

quizBank.question: A chat server crashes while 50,000 users are connected. What happens to their messages and connections?
quizBank.answer: Connections are dropped immediately — clients get disconnection events. Client should auto-reconnect with exponential backoff. Messages in flight (published to Kafka but not delivered) are safe — Kafka retains them. On reconnect, client sends last_seen_message_id, server replays missed messages from Cassandra. For offline duration, missed messages are retrieved from message history. The crashed server's load is redistributed via load balancer to remaining servers.

quizBank.question: What does 'delivered' status mean vs 'read' status in WhatsApp?
options: Delivered means reached server; Read means recipient opened the message
Delivered means recipient's device received it; Read means they saw it
Delivered means sent; Read means downloaded
quizBank.answer: Delivered means recipient's device received it; Read means they saw it
quizBank.explanation: Three states: Sent (server ACK), Delivered (recipient device ACK via WebSocket), Read (recipient opened chat and message was visible). Each requires an explicit ACK from the recipient's client.

quizBank.question: You have a group chat with 100,000 members (think a public community). Someone sends a message. How do you fan-out?
quizBank.answer: Fan-out on write would generate 100,000 WebSocket pushes per message — unacceptable. Use fan-out on read: store message once in DB. When a member opens the chat, they pull messages since their last_read_id. For online members who have the chat open, push in real-time but batch: segment members by chat server and push once per server (each server then delivers to its connected members). Prioritize: push to members who are actively viewing, lazy-load for background.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'message ordering' when messages arrive out of order due to network issues?
quizBank.answer: Use client-side sequence numbers per conversation. Each message includes: client-generated temp_id, server-assigned message_id (Snowflake, time-ordered), conversation_id, and seq_num (monotonic counter per conversation). Client sorts by message_id (Snowflake contains timestamp). If gap detected (seq_num jump), client requests missing messages from server. Server stores in Cassandra ordered by message_id DESC — reads always return correctly ordered messages.

quizBank.question: How many WebSocket servers are needed for 100 million concurrent connections, if each server handles 50,000 connections?
quizBank.answer: 2,000 servers
quizBank.explanation: 100M / 50,000 = 2,000 servers. In practice, add 20% buffer = 2,400 servers. Modern servers with event-loop architecture (Go, Node.js) can handle 50,000-100,000 WebSocket connections per server.

quizBank.question: A user receives 1,000 notifications while their phone is off. When they turn it on, what should happen?
quizBank.answer: Mobile OS batches and delivers push notifications (FCM/APNS handles queuing during offline). App receives push notification with badge count. On app open, client connects via WebSocket and sends last_seen_message_id per conversation. Server queries Cassandra for messages after that ID. Client renders 'X unread messages' badge. Individual notifications are collapsed (FCM supports notification collapse keys) to avoid notification flooding.

quizBank.question: Explain how you would implement end-to-end encryption for group messages.
quizBank.answer: Use Signal Protocol's Sender Keys: (1) Group creator generates a random Sender Key (group encryption key). (2) Sender Key is encrypted with each member's public key (X3DH key exchange) and distributed to all members. (3) Messages are encrypted with the Sender Key — only once per message regardless of group size. (4) Server stores only ciphertext — cannot read messages. (5) When a member joins/leaves: rotate Sender Key, re-distribute to current members. (6) Key management: each device has its own key pair. The server stores encrypted key bundles.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a Hybrid Logical Clock (HLC) in a distributed chat system?
options: To assign causally consistent timestamps that capture message ordering across nodes
quizBank.answer: To assign causally consistent timestamps that capture message ordering across nodes
quizBank.explanation: Lamport clocks provide causal ordering but not wall-clock time. Physical clocks drift. HLC combines both: max(physical_time, logical_time) + counter. This gives messages timestamps that are close to real time and causally consistent, enabling correct ordering even across distributed servers.

quizBank.question: How do you handle typing indicators without overloading the server?
quizBank.answer: Throttle: client sends 'typing start' event once, then suppresses further events for 3 seconds. After 5 seconds of no typing, send 'typing stop'. Server fan-outs to conversation members via Redis Pub/Sub. Do NOT persist typing events to DB — pure in-memory. Rate limit: max 1 typing event per user per conversation per 3 seconds. For large groups (>20 members), aggregate: 'Alice and 3 others are typing' rather than individual indicators.

quizBank.question: What is the role of Kafka in the chat message pipeline?
options: Decouple message processing: persistence, notifications, analytics
quizBank.answer: Decouple message processing: persistence, notifications, analytics
quizBank.explanation: Kafka is a durable event bus. Chat server publishes message events to Kafka. Multiple consumers process independently: Message Store writes to Cassandra, Notification Service sends push notifications, Analytics Service logs events. This decoupling enables independent scaling and failure isolation.

quizBank.question: Design the unread message count feature. How do you efficiently show '5 unread' per conversation?
quizBank.answer: Per-member approach: store last_read_message_id in conversation_members table. Unread count = SELECT COUNT(*) FROM messages WHERE conversation_id=? AND message_id > last_read_message_id. This is expensive at scale. Better: maintain a Redis counter per (user, conversation): INCR unread:{user_id}:{conv_id} on new message, RESET to 0 on read. Trade-off: Redis counter can drift if messages are deleted. Periodic reconciliation query from DB as ground truth.

quizBank.question: Your Kafka cluster goes down. What happens to message delivery?
quizBank.answer: Messages still flow via WebSocket (real-time delivery is not Kafka-dependent in the primary path). Kafka outage affects: (1) Message persistence — messages not written to Cassandra, risk of data loss for delivered-but-not-persisted messages. (2) Push notifications — offline users don't get notified. (3) Analytics — events dropped. Mitigation: Circuit breaker — if Kafka down, write directly to DB (synchronous, higher latency). Buffer events in local memory, replay when Kafka recovers. Alert on Kafka consumer lag.

quizBank.question: What Cassandra partition key design supports efficient message retrieval for a conversation?
options: Partition by conversation_id, cluster by message_id
Partition by date, cluster by conversation_id
quizBank.answer: Partition by conversation_id, cluster by message_id
quizBank.explanation: All messages for a conversation are in the same partition (fast range scans by time). Clustering by message_id (Snowflake, time-ordered) enables efficient pagination queries: 'give me 50 messages before message_id X'. Partitioning by user_id would scatter group messages.

quizBank.question: How would you design message search ('find all messages containing hello') at scale?
quizBank.answer: Do NOT query Cassandra with LIKE — it cannot do full-text search efficiently. Options: (1) Elasticsearch: index messages in near-real-time. Search by user's conversations + keyword. Expensive storage. (2) Apache Solr with conversation-level sharding. (3) For privacy (E2E encrypted): client-side search — store encrypted index on device, search locally. WhatsApp uses client-side search for this reason. For non-E2E: Elasticsearch with conversation_id filter, paginated results, highlight matching text.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between push notifications and WebSocket delivery? When do you use each?
quizBank.answer: WebSocket: app is open and connected. Message delivered instantly via persistent connection. No involvement of FCM/APNS. Push notifications: app is in background or closed. Server sends to FCM/APNS → OS delivers notification to device. Wakes app if configured. Use WebSocket for active users; push notification for offline/background users. Check presence before deciding which path to use. Many systems do both in parallel: WebSocket for immediate delivery, push notification as fallback.

quizBank.question: What mechanism prevents a message from being delivered twice if the sender retries due to network timeout?
quizBank.answer: Idempotency keys (unique message_id per message)
quizBank.explanation: Client generates a unique message_id (UUID or Snowflake) before sending. Server does INSERT IF NOT EXISTS (or upsert) by message_id. If client retries the same message, the second insert is a no-op. The server returns the same result. This achieves exactly-once delivery semantics.

quizBank.question: A chat server needs to be deployed with zero downtime. How do you drain WebSocket connections?
quizBank.answer: Graceful shutdown procedure: (1) Remove server from load balancer (stop new connections). (2) Send a 'reconnect' signal to all connected clients via WebSocket. (3) Clients reconnect to other servers (they get routed to different servers by LB). (4) Wait for all connections to drop (timeout: 30 seconds). (5) Shut down process. Load balancer health check fails → server is removed from rotation. Redis user→server mapping is updated when clients reconnect. Total downtime for individual users: <1 second.

quizBank.question: How do you handle the case where two users send messages to each other at the exact same millisecond?
quizBank.answer: Network non-determinism means message ordering can be ambiguous. Solutions: (1) Snowflake IDs have sub-millisecond resolution (12-bit sequence counter within same millisecond). Messages from different servers still get unique IDs but relative order is ambiguous. (2) Use conversation-level sequence numbers: single Redis INCR per conversation ensures strict monotonic ordering. Trade-off: Redis becomes a bottleneck for high-frequency conversations. (3) Accept eventual consistency: display both messages, let Cassandra's row ordering by message_id determine display order. Users perceive this as simultaneous — acceptable UX.

quizBank.question: What is the 'session affinity' or 'sticky session' problem with WebSocket servers?
options: Users can only connect to one server forever
Standard load balancers route WebSocket upgrades incorrectly
WebSocket connections are stateful — user must stay connected to same server
WebSocket requires more CPU than HTTP
quizBank.answer: WebSocket connections are stateful — user must stay connected to same server
quizBank.explanation: Unlike HTTP (stateless), a WebSocket connection is a persistent stateful connection to a specific server. The load balancer must route the initial HTTP upgrade request and then maintain that connection to the same server. Use IP hash or cookie-based routing in LB, or use a Layer 7 LB that understands WebSocket.

cheatSheet:
# Design a News Feed (Facebook/Twitter)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Users can post updates (text, images, videos)
- Users see a personalized feed of posts from people they follow
- Feed is ranked (not chronological) by relevance/engagement
- Real-time updates: new posts appear without page refresh
- Pagination: infinite scroll
- Like, comment, share interactions
- Hashtags and mentions

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Low latency feed load: <200ms p99
- High availability: 99.99%
- Eventual consistency acceptable (feed can be slightly stale)
- Scale: 1B DAU, 500M posts/day, each user follows avg 200 people

## 2. Capacity Estimation

### Traffic
- Post writes: 500M/day = ~5,800 writes/sec
- Feed reads: assume each user reads feed 10x/day = 10B reads/day = ~116,000 reads/sec
- Like/comment events: ~2B/day = ~23,000 events/sec

### Fan-out
- User with 1M followers posts → 1M feed updates needed (fan-out on write)
- Average 200 followers: 5,800 posts/sec x 200 = 1.16M feed updates/sec
- Celebrity problem: 1 post → 1M updates in seconds

### Storage
- Post: ~1KB (text + metadata)
- 500M posts/day x 1KB x 365 = ~183TB/year for posts
- Feed cache: store 500 posts per user in Redis: 1B users x 500 x 8 bytes (post_id) = 4TB RAM (just IDs)
- Media: 10% posts with 1MB media = 50M x 1MB = 50TB/day

## 3. Fan-out Strategies

### Fan-out on Write (Push Model)
- When user posts, immediately push post_id to all followers' feed caches
- Pros: fast feed read (pre-computed), simple read path
- Cons: huge write amplification for celebrities, wasted work for inactive users

### Fan-out on Read (Pull Model)
- Feed is computed at read time: fetch posts from all followed users
- Pros: no write amplification, always fresh
- Cons: high read latency (must query N users' timelines), complex aggregation

### Hybrid Model (Recommended)
- Normal users (< 10K followers): fan-out on write
- Celebrities (> 10K followers): fan-out on read
- Active users: pre-compute feed
- Inactive users (>30 days offline): skip fan-out, compute on next login

```mermaid
graph TD
    User([User Posts])
    PostService[Post Service]
    PostDB[(Post Store - MySQL Sharded)]
    MQ[Kafka - post_events]
    FanoutWorker[Fan-out Worker]
    FollowerService[Follower Service]
    FeedCache[(Redis Feed Cache per user)]
    CelebPosts[(Celebrity Post Index)]
    FeedService[Feed Service]
    RankingEngine[Ranking Engine ML]
    Client([Client - Read Feed])
    CDN[CDN - Media]
    ObjectStore[S3 - Media]

    User -->|POST /post| PostService
    PostService --> PostDB
    PostService --> MQ
    MQ --> FanoutWorker
    FanoutWorker --> FollowerService
    FanoutWorker -->|Normal users| FeedCache
    FeedCache --> FeedService
    CelebPosts --> FeedService
    FeedService --> RankingEngine
    RankingEngine --> Client
    PostDB -->|On read miss| FeedService
    ObjectStore --> CDN
    CDN --> Client
```

## 4. Feed Ranking Algorithm

### Signals Used
- **Affinity**: how often do you interact with this person?
- **Weight**: post type (video > image > text), recency
- **Time decay**: older posts score lower exponentially
- **Engagement velocity**: posts getting rapid likes/shares boosted
- **User preferences**: topics/hashtags user engages with

### Score Formula (simplified)
```
score = affinity_score * type_weight * time_decay(age_hours) * engagement_velocity
time_decay = e^(-age_hours / 24)
```

### Ranking Architecture
- Candidate generation: fetch top 500 candidate posts from feed cache
- Scoring: ML model assigns score to each candidate
- Filtering: remove blocked users, already-seen posts, spam
- Return top 20-50 posts per page

## 5. Data Model

### Posts Table (MySQL, sharded by user_id)
```sql
CREATE TABLE posts (
    post_id     BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,  -- Snowflake ID
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    content     TEXT,
    media_urls  JSON,
    post_type   ENUM('text','image','video'),
    like_count  INT DEFAULT 0,
    comment_count INT DEFAULT 0,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    is_deleted  BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);
CREATE INDEX idx_user_posts ON posts(user_id, created_at DESC);
```

### Social Graph (follows) — Neo4j or MySQL
```sql
CREATE TABLE follows (
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_followees ON follows(followee_id);  -- who follows celebrity X
```

### Feed Cache (Redis)
```
Key: feed:{user_id}
Type: Sorted Set
Score: post_rank_score (or timestamp for chronological)
Member: post_id
Capacity: 500 post_ids per user
```

## 6. API Design

```
POST /api/v1/posts
Body: { "content": "...", "media_ids": [...], "visibility": "public" }
Response 201: { "post_id": "...", "created_at": "..." }

GET /api/v1/feed?cursor={last_seen_post_id}&limit=20
Response 200: { "posts": [...], "next_cursor": "...", "has_more": true }

GET /api/v1/users/{user_id}/posts?before={post_id}&limit=20
Response 200: { "posts": [...] }

POST /api/v1/posts/{post_id}/like
Response 200: { "like_count": 1523 }

GET /api/v1/posts/{post_id}/comments?cursor={cursor}&limit=20
Response 200: { "comments": [...], "next_cursor": "..." }

GET /api/v1/trending?hashtag={tag}
Response 200: { "posts": [...] }
```

## 7. Real-Time Feed Updates

### Options
- **Polling**: Client polls /feed every 30 seconds. Simple, high latency, wasteful.
- **Long polling**: Client holds connection. Server responds when new post arrives. Better latency.
- **Server-Sent Events (SSE)**: Server pushes new post IDs to client. Client fetches full post. Recommended.
- **WebSocket**: Overkill for feed (server-to-client only needed).

### SSE-based Real-Time Updates
- Client connects: `GET /api/v1/feed/stream`
- Server holds SSE connection
- When new post arrives for user (fan-out complete), push `data: {"new_post_id": "..."}`
- Client fetches full post details and prepends to feed

## 8. Pagination Strategy

### Cursor-based Pagination (Recommended)
- Use post_id (Snowflake, time-ordered) as cursor
- `GET /feed?cursor=12345&limit=20` → returns posts where post_id < 12345
- Stable: not affected by new posts being added to top of feed
- Efficient: index seek on post_id, no OFFSET

### Why not OFFSET pagination?
- `LIMIT 20 OFFSET 1000` scans 1020 rows, returns last 20 — O(n) inefficiency
- Unstable: new posts shift pages, causing duplicates or skipped posts

## 9. Media Handling

### Upload Flow
1. Client requests presigned S3 URL from Post Service
2. Client uploads directly to S3 (large file, no round-trip through app servers)
3. S3 triggers Lambda → generates thumbnails, transcodes video
4. Media URL stored in post record
5. CDN caches media with long TTL (media is immutable)

## 10. Scalability & Bottlenecks

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Celebrity (10M followers)
    participant PS as Post Service
    participant K as Kafka
    participant FW as Fan-out Workers (pool)
    participant RC as Redis Cluster
    participant FS as Feed Service
    participant U as User A

    C->>PS: Post new update
    PS->>K: Publish post_event
    Note over K,FW: Celebrity: skip fan-out on write
    K->>FW: Fan-out to normal followers only
    FW->>RC: ZADD feed:{follower_id} score post_id (bulk)
    U->>FS: GET /feed
    FS->>RC: ZRANGE feed:{user_id} (pre-built for normal users)
    FS->>PS: Fetch celebrity posts (on-demand)
    FS-->>U: Merged, ranked feed
```

- **Like counter**: use Redis INCR (in-memory), batch-write to DB periodically to avoid DB hammering
- **Fan-out lag**: for celebrities, workers process in parallel by partitioning follower list
- **Feed cache eviction**: LRU, keep only 500 post IDs per user
- **Cold start**: new user with no follows → show trending/recommended posts

## 11. Monitoring
- Feed load latency p50/p95/p99
- Fan-out queue depth (Kafka lag)
- Feed cache hit rate (target >95%)
- Post write throughput
- Like/comment event processing lag
- ML ranking model inference latency

quizBank.question: What is 'fan-out on write' in the context of a news feed?
options: When a post is created, immediately updating all followers' feed caches
Writing media to multiple CDN nodes
quizBank.answer: When a post is created, immediately updating all followers' feed caches
quizBank.explanation: Fan-out on write (push model): when a user posts, the system proactively pushes the post_id to all followers' pre-computed feeds. This makes reads fast (pre-built) but writes expensive (write amplification).

quizBank.question: A celebrity has 50 million followers and posts 10 times per day. Fan-out on write generates how many feed updates per day?
quizBank.explanation: 50M followers x 10 posts/day = 500M feed update operations per day just for one celebrity. This is why celebrities use fan-out on read instead.

quizBank.question: Why is cursor-based pagination preferred over offset-based for news feeds?
options: Offset pagination becomes unstable when new items are inserted at the top
quizBank.answer: Offset pagination becomes unstable when new items are inserted at the top
quizBank.explanation: With LIMIT 20 OFFSET 20, if a new post is added to the top, page 2 now contains one item from old page 1. Users see duplicates or miss posts. Cursor-based pagination (use last seen post_id as anchor) is stable regardless of insertions.

quizBank.question: A user follows both Taylor Swift (50M followers) and a friend (100 followers). How do you merge their posts into one ranked feed?
quizBank.answer: Hybrid approach: Friend's posts are in user's pre-built feed cache (fan-out on write, since friend has few followers). Taylor Swift's posts are fetched on-demand (fan-out on read). Feed Service: (1) Read user's feed cache from Redis — contains friend posts and other normal followees. (2) Fetch Taylor Swift's recent posts from her post timeline. (3) Merge both lists, run through ranking model, return top N. The merge happens at read time, but only for celebrity posts.

quizBank.question: What data structure in Redis is best for storing a user's feed (ordered list of post IDs)?
quizBank.answer: Sorted Set
quizBank.explanation: Redis Sorted Set stores members with a score. For feeds: member = post_id, score = rank_score or timestamp. ZRANGE/ZREVRANGE retrieves posts in order efficiently. Enables cursor-based pagination by score. ZADD is O(log N).

quizBank.question: Your Kafka fan-out workers fall behind during a viral event. Followers see the post 10 minutes late. How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Prioritize fan-out queue: move high-engagement posts to a priority Kafka partition processed faster. Horizontal scaling: auto-scale fan-out workers when queue depth exceeds threshold. Shed load: for users inactive >7 days, skip fan-out (they'll see it when they log in via pull). Circuit breaker: if fan-out lag >5 min, switch to hybrid pull for that event. Real-time path: push to actively online users via SSE immediately (separate fast path), while Kafka handles persistence and offline users.

quizBank.question: How do you efficiently count likes on a post when 1 million people like it within 1 minute?
quizBank.answer: Do not write to DB for every like — that's 1M writes/min = 16,667 writes/sec on a single counter. Solution: Redis INCR: INCR likes:{post_id}. Redis handles 100K+ ops/sec in-memory. Background worker reads Redis counters and batch-writes to DB every 5-10 seconds. Trade-off: DB count may be slightly stale (max 10 seconds). For display: always read from Redis. For analytics: DB is source of truth. Handle unlike: DECR in Redis. Handle idempotency: maintain SET liked_by:{post_id} in Redis to prevent double-like (or check DB).

quizBank.question: What is the 'thundering herd' problem in a news feed system?
options: Too many writes hitting the database
When a celebrity posts and millions of users refresh their feed simultaneously
Cache eviction causing all requests to hit DB at once
Load balancer overwhelmed during traffic spikes
quizBank.answer: Cache eviction causing all requests to hit DB at once
quizBank.explanation: Thundering herd: a popular cached item expires → all concurrent requests miss cache simultaneously → all hit DB at once → DB overloaded. Prevention: use cache lock (Redis SETNX), probabilistic early expiration, or cache warming before expiry.

quizBank.question: How would you design the 'who liked this post' feature showing mutual friends first?
quizBank.answer: Store likes in a table: post_likes(post_id, user_id, created_at). Query: SELECT l.user_id FROM post_likes l JOIN follows f ON l.user_id = f.followee_id WHERE l.post_id=? AND f.follower_id=current_user ORDER BY l.created_at DESC LIMIT 3. Problem at scale: post with 1M likes — this join is expensive. Solution: Cache top likers per post in Redis (List). For mutual friends: precompute friend graph in Redis Set, intersect with likers Set: SINTERSTORE result follows:{user_id} likers:{post_id}. Return intersection first, then others.

quizBank.question: How do you prevent a user from seeing posts they've already seen when infinite scrolling loads the next page?
quizBank.answer: Cursor-based pagination by post_id prevents seeing the same page, but new posts inserted at top can't shift already-delivered cursors. Session-specific feed snapshot: generate feed snapshot for user session (list of post_ids), store in Redis with session TTL. Paginate through the snapshot — stable ordering regardless of new posts. Trade-off: feed feels stale within a session. Refresh snapshot on explicit user pull-to-refresh. Seen post tracking: maintain SEEN set per user in Redis, filter out seen post_ids. Expensive at scale (1000 posts/user = 8KB per user x 1B users = 8TB).

quizBank.question: Which signal is most important for ranking a post higher in the news feed?
quizBank.answer: User affinity (past interactions with poster)
quizBank.explanation: Affinity captures the relationship strength between viewer and poster. If you frequently like/comment on someone's posts, you have high affinity — their posts rank higher. Time decay is also important but affinity is the primary signal for personalization.

quizBank.question: A user deactivates their account. How should this affect the news feed of their 10,000 followers?
quizBank.answer: Immediate: mark user as deactivated in user table. Post Service: filter deactivated users' posts from feed. Problem: their post_ids are already in followers' feed caches in Redis. Options: (1) Lazy filtering: check poster's status at hydration time — if deactivated, skip post. (2) Eager cleanup: background job removes their post_ids from all followers' feed caches (expensive: 10K Redis operations). (3) Hybrid: lazy filter (cheaper) + async cache cleanup. Deleted posts: similar approach. Privacy requirement may demand immediate removal → use eager approach despite cost.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between a chronological feed and an algorithmic feed? What are the trade-offs?
quizBank.answer: Chronological: posts displayed in order of creation time. Simple, predictable, users see everything from everyone they follow. Problem: heavy followees drown out light ones; high-volume posters dominate; misses relevance. Algorithmic: ML model ranks posts by predicted engagement. Pros: shows most relevant content, better engagement, compensates for follow imbalance. Cons: creates 'filter bubble', unpredictable, users feel they miss posts, complex to build and maintain, potential for bias amplification. Many platforms (Twitter/X) now offer both options — algorithmic default with chronological toggle.

quizBank.question: How should media uploads be handled to avoid overloading the post service?
options: Stream media through post service to S3
Use multipart upload directly to S3 via presigned URL
Store media in the database as BLOB
Use a CDN as the primary upload target
quizBank.answer: Use multipart upload directly to S3 via presigned URL
quizBank.explanation: Direct S3 upload: client gets presigned URL from server (fast, lightweight request), uploads directly to S3 (large, bandwidth-intensive). Post service never touches the media bytes. S3 triggers Lambda for transcoding. This scales media upload independently from post service.

quizBank.question: Your Redis cluster storing feed caches goes down. What is the fallback strategy?
quizBank.answer: Circuit breaker triggers: feed service detects Redis unavailability, switches to fallback mode. Fallback: compute feed on-the-fly from DB (pull model). For each request: get followees from follow table, query posts DB for recent posts, sort by time, return. This is slower (500-1000ms vs 10ms) but functional. DB read replicas handle the increased load. Shed load: return cached HTTP responses where possible. Graceful degradation: show chronological feed instead of ranked. Alert on-call. Rebuild cache from DB when Redis recovers using backfill job.

quizBank.question: How would you implement hashtag-based trending topics?
quizBank.answer: Extract hashtags from posts at write time. Kafka consumer processes post events, extracts hashtags. Increment counters in Redis: ZINCRBY trending:hourly {score} {hashtag}. Decay score over time (Hacker News-style: score / (age_hours + 2)^1.5). Trending list: ZREVRANGE trending:hourly 0 9 (top 10). Refresh every 5 minutes. For global trends: aggregate Redis counts across shards. Geo-specific trending: separate sorted sets per country. Display: show post count, example posts. Storage: hourly snapshots to DynamoDB for historical analysis.

quizBank.question: What approach does Facebook use to serve a single user's news feed from pre-computed data?
options: Dynamic SQL query on every request
Pre-computed feed stored in Redis sorted set, hydrated with full post data on read
Store full post content in Redis
Query each followed user's timeline separately and merge
quizBank.answer: Pre-computed feed stored in Redis sorted set, hydrated with full post data on read
quizBank.explanation: Store only post_ids in Redis sorted set (memory efficient). On read: ZREVRANGE gives ordered post_ids, then batch-fetch full post data from post store (in-memory cache or DB). This two-phase approach separates feed ordering (in Redis) from post data fetching (from post cache).

quizBank.question: How do you ensure a user never sees a post they blocked or from a user they blocked?
quizBank.answer: Block list stored in Redis Set: blocked:{user_id} = {blocked_user_ids}. At fan-out: skip adding posts to feed if poster is in blocker's block list. At read time: filter function checks each post_id's user_id against block list. Both checks needed: (1) Existing posts in feed cache from before block — need read-time filter. (2) New posts after block — prevented by fan-out filter. Mutual block: neither sees the other. Block list in Redis: SISMEMBER blocked:{user_id} poster_id. Propagate block event via Kafka to feed service to clear cached posts from blocked user.

quizBank.question: How would you design A/B testing for news feed ranking algorithms?
quizBank.answer: User bucketing: deterministically assign users to experiment groups (e.g., hash(user_id) % 100 < 10 → treatment group). Bucketing persisted in user table or feature flag service (LaunchDarkly). Treatment: different group → different ranking model. Model serving: feature flag in Feed Service routes to model A or model B. Metrics: track per-group: CTR, time spent, scroll depth, like rate, unfollow rate. Statistical significance: minimum detectable effect, required sample size calculated upfront. Guard rails: if session time drops >5%, auto-rollback. Infrastructure: both models run in parallel, model inference service routes by experiment group.

quizBank.question: What technique avoids the N+1 query problem when hydrating a list of 50 post IDs from cache?
options: Fetch posts one by one in a loop
Batch fetch: single query/mget with all post IDs
quizBank.answer: Batch fetch: single query/mget with all post IDs
quizBank.explanation: N+1 problem: for 50 post_ids, making 50 individual queries = 50 round trips. Batch fetch: Redis MGET [key1, key2, ..., key50] = 1 round trip. Or SQL: WHERE post_id IN (id1, id2, ..., id50). This reduces feed hydration from 50 serial round trips to 1 parallel batch fetch.

quizBank.question: A new user signs up and follows 0 people. What do you show in their empty feed?
quizBank.answer: Cold start problem. Options: (1) Onboarding wizard: prompt to follow suggested accounts based on interests selected during signup. (2) Trending content: show globally/regionally trending posts. (3) Explore/discovery feed: content from accounts similar to what user browsed (implicit signals). (4) Friend suggestions: 'People you may know' based on phone contacts, mutual follows, or email. (5) Interest-based recommendations: user selects topics during signup, show top posts in those topics. Priority: get user to follow ≥5 accounts ASAP — feed quality improves dramatically. Metric: time to first follow after signup.

quizBank.question: How would you handle the news feed for a user who follows 10,000 accounts (power user)?
quizBank.answer: Fan-out on write for 10,000 followees is fine on the write side (normal users fan-out to this user's cache). But read side: if reading 10,000 accounts' posts — too many candidates. Optimization: (1) Cap candidate pool: only fetch from top 200 most-interacted followees (affinity score). (2) For others: show posts only if they reach viral threshold (high engagement velocity). (3) Paginate through followees in batches, stop when enough candidates found. (4) Feed cache: store top 200 post_ids. User can switch to 'All Following' view which triggers full pull. (5) Personalized muting: user can mute high-volume accounts without unfollowing.

cheatSheet:
# Design a Rate Limiter

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Limit requests per user/IP/API key within a time window
- Return HTTP 429 Too Many Requests when limit exceeded
- Different limits per API endpoint (e.g., /login: 5/min, /search: 100/min)
- Whitelist/blacklist support
- Soft limits (warn) vs hard limits (block)

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Ultra-low latency: must not add >1ms to request processing
- High availability: rate limiter failure should fail open (not block all traffic)
- Distributed: works across multiple servers
- Accurate: minimal false positives/negatives
- Scale: handle 1M+ requests/sec

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- 1M requests/sec through rate limiter
- Each check: 1 Redis operation (~0.1ms)
- Redis throughput: 100K-1M ops/sec per node
- Need Redis Cluster with 3-5 nodes for 1M req/sec
- Memory per user: ~100 bytes in sliding window log → 10M users = 1GB RAM
- Token bucket: 40 bytes per user → 10M users = 400MB RAM

## 3. Rate Limiting Algorithms

### Algorithm 1: Token Bucket
- Each user has a bucket with capacity N tokens
- Tokens refill at rate R tokens/second
- Each request consumes 1 token; if empty → reject
- Allows burst up to bucket capacity
- Simple, memory efficient (store: token_count, last_refill_time)

```
state = GET bucket:{user_id}
tokens, last_refill = state
now = current_time()
time_passed = now - last_refill
new_tokens = min(capacity, tokens + time_passed * refill_rate)
if new_tokens >= 1:
    SET bucket:{user_id} (new_tokens - 1, now)
    ALLOW
else:
    REJECT
```

### Algorithm 2: Leaky Bucket
- Requests enter a FIFO queue (the bucket)
- Processed at a fixed rate (the leak)
- Excess requests overflow and are dropped
- Smooths out bursts, consistent output rate
- Used for network traffic shaping

### Algorithm 3: Fixed Window Counter
- Count requests in fixed time windows (e.g., 1-minute buckets)
- INCR counter:{user_id}:{window} → if > limit, reject
- Problem: boundary burst — user sends 100 at end of window 1 + 100 at start of window 2 = 200 in 1 minute
- Simple, fast, but inaccurate at window boundaries

### Algorithm 4: Sliding Window Log
- Store timestamp of each request in a sorted set
- Remove timestamps older than window
- Count remaining = current requests in window
- Accurate but memory-intensive (store all timestamps)

### Algorithm 5: Sliding Window Counter (Recommended)
- Hybrid: uses fixed window counts + weighted interpolation
- `count = prev_window_count * overlap_ratio + current_window_count`
- Accurate (~0.003% error), memory efficient
- Example: window = 1 min, limit = 100. If 30s into current window: prev_count * 0.5 + curr_count

```mermaid
graph TD
    Req([Incoming Request])
    MW[Rate Limiter Middleware]
    Redis[Redis Cluster]
    Allow[Forward to API Server]
    Reject[Return 429]
    Rules[Rules Service - limits config]

    Req --> MW
    MW --> Rules
    Rules --> MW
    MW -->|Check & update counter| Redis
    Redis -->|Under limit| Allow
    Redis -->|Over limit| Reject
    Allow --> API[API Server]
```

## 4. Distributed Rate Limiting

### Problem with Local Rate Limiting
- 3 servers, limit = 5 req/min per user
- User sends 5 to Server 1, 5 to Server 2, 5 to Server 3 = 15 requests pass
- Each server thinks it saw only 5

### Solution: Centralized Redis
- All servers check/update same Redis counter
- Atomic operations: INCR + EXPIRE (Lua script for atomicity)
- Adds ~0.5-1ms network latency

### Redis Lua Script (atomic sliding window)
```lua
local key = KEYS[1]
local window = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local limit = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])

-- Remove old entries
redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, 0, now - window)
-- Count current
local count = redis.call('ZCARD', key)
if count < limit then
    -- Add current request
    redis.call('ZADD', key, now, now)
    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, window)
    return 1  -- allow
else
    return 0  -- reject
end
```

### Race Condition Fix
- Without Lua: ZCARD → check → ZADD is not atomic → two requests can both see count < limit
- Lua script runs atomically in Redis single-threaded executor

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S1 as Server 1
    participant S2 as Server 2
    participant R as Redis Cluster
    participant API as API Service

    C->>S1: Request (load balanced)
    S1->>R: EVALSHA lua_script user_id window limit now
    R-->>S1: 1 (allowed)
    S1->>API: Forward request
    API-->>S1: Response
    S1-->>C: 200 OK + headers
    Note over S1: X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
    Note over S1: X-RateLimit-Remaining: 47
    Note over S1: X-RateLimit-Reset: 1700000060
    C->>S2: Next Request
    S2->>R: EVALSHA lua_script user_id window limit now
    R-->>S2: 0 (rejected - limit reached)
    S2-->>C: 429 Too Many Requests
```

## 5. Multi-Tier Rate Limiting

```
Tier 1: IP-level rate limit (at CDN/load balancer) — blocks DDoS
Tier 2: User-level rate limit (in middleware) — per-authenticated user
Tier 3: API-key level — per third-party client
Tier 4: Endpoint-specific — /login stricter than /search
```

### Rules Configuration
```json
{
  "rules": [
    { "key": "ip", "endpoint": "*", "limit": 1000, "window": 60 },
    { "key": "user_id", "endpoint": "/api/v1/search", "limit": 100, "window": 60 },
    { "key": "user_id", "endpoint": "/api/v1/login", "limit": 5, "window": 60 },
    { "key": "api_key", "endpoint": "*", "limit": 10000, "window": 3600 }
  ]
}
```

## 6. API Design

```
All rate-limited responses include headers:
X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 47
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1700000060  (Unix timestamp when window resets)
Retry-After: 30               (seconds until next allowed request)

HTTP 429 Response Body:
{ "error": "rate_limit_exceeded", "message": "Too many requests", "retry_after": 30 }

Admin API:
GET  /admin/rate-limit/rules              — list all rules
POST /admin/rate-limit/rules              — create rule
PUT  /admin/rate-limit/rules/{id}         — update rule
DEL  /admin/rate-limit/rules/{id}         — delete rule
POST /admin/rate-limit/whitelist/{user}   — whitelist user
GET  /admin/rate-limit/status/{user_id}   — current counter state
```

## 7. Data Model

### Redis Keys
```
Fixed window:    rl:fw:{user_id}:{endpoint_hash}:{window_start}  → INT counter
Sliding window:  rl:sw:{user_id}:{endpoint_hash}               → Sorted Set (score=timestamp)
Token bucket:    rl:tb:{user_id}:{endpoint_hash}               → Hash {tokens, last_refill}
Whitelist:       rl:whitelist                                   → Set of user_ids
Blacklist:       rl:blacklist                                   → Set of IPs
```

### Rules DB (PostgreSQL)
```sql
CREATE TABLE rate_limit_rules (
    id          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    key_type    VARCHAR(20),   -- ip, user_id, api_key
    endpoint    VARCHAR(255),  -- /api/v1/search or *
    limit_count INT,
    window_secs INT,
    is_active   BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

## 8. Fail Open vs Fail Closed

- **Fail open** (recommended for most APIs): if Redis is down, allow requests through. Availability > perfect limiting.
- **Fail closed**: if Redis is down, block all requests. Use for security-critical endpoints (login, payment).
- Implement via circuit breaker: track Redis error rate; above threshold, switch to local in-memory fallback limiter.

## 9. Scalability Considerations

- **Redis Cluster**: shard by user_id hash. Each shard handles subset of users.
- **Local cache**: cache rate limit rules in app server memory (refresh every 60s) to avoid Redis for rule lookups.
- **Async counter**: for non-critical limits, allow slight over-counting — INCR locally, sync to Redis every 100ms. Reduces Redis load by 100x.
- **CDN-level limiting**: Cloudflare/Akamai support rate limiting at edge — blocks traffic before it reaches origin.

## 10. Monitoring
- Rate limit hit rate per endpoint (% of requests rejected)
- Redis latency for rate limit checks (p99 target: <1ms)
- False positive rate (legitimate users blocked)
- Whitelist/blacklist effectiveness
- Redis memory utilization
