levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows ride-sharing as driver-rider matching. Can describe basic flow: request → match → drive → pay.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands geospatial challenges. Can describe geohash. Knows about ETA calculation.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs full matching pipeline. Implements geospatial Redis index. Handles neighbor cells. Designs WebSocket tracking. Surge pricing basics.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Handles scale: 500K driver updates/sec, distributed geospatial index, road-network ETA, driver rejection cascade, heatmap for surge.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs H3/S2 hierarchical indexing, ML-based demand prediction for proactive driver positioning, multi-modal routing, safety features, and global multi-city architecture.
skills: ML demand prediction for driver supply

plan.overview: 10-session plan covering geospatial indexing, matching, real-time tracking, and surge pricing.

sessions.title: Requirements & Core Flow
sessions.goals: Scope the system. Understand the core request-match-trip flow.
activities: Draw the rider and driver journey.
Identify the hardest problem: real-time geospatial matching.
Estimate scale: drivers, requests, location updates.

sessions.title: Geospatial Indexing
sessions.goals: Master geohash and neighbor cell queries.
activities: Understand geohash encoding: lat/lng → string prefix.
Query geohash cell + 8 neighbors for boundary cases.
Redis Hash: HSET drivers:{geohash} driver_id data.

sessions.title: Driver Location Pipeline
activities: Driver app sends update every 5 seconds.
Location Service: update Redis geospatial index.
Async: publish to Kafka → Cassandra history.
Redis Cluster: shard by geohash prefix.

sessions.title: Driver Matching Algorithm
activities: Find candidate drivers via geohash query.
Score candidates: ETA + rating + acceptance rate.
Handle driver rejection: cascade to next.

sessions.title: Real-Time Tracking (WebSocket)
sessions.goals: Show live driver position to rider.
activities: Driver location update → Redis Pub/Sub → rider WebSocket.
Interpolation between GPS updates for smooth animation.

sessions.title: Data Model & Trip State Machine
activities: Trip state machine: requested → matched → arriving → in_progress → completed.

sessions.title: Edge Cases & Failure Handling
activities: No drivers nearby: expand radius or queue.
Driver doesn't respond: 15-second timeout, next driver.

sessions.title: Mock Interview
activities: Must cover: geospatial index, matching flow, real-time tracking, surge.
Discuss: geohash vs S2, WebSocket vs polling.

quizBank.question: Why is Euclidean (straight-line) distance insufficient for ETA calculation in a ride-sharing app?
options: It ignores roads, traffic, one-way streets — actual driving distance is always longer than straight-line
It doesn't work with GPS coordinates
It can only calculate distances under 10km
quizBank.answer: It ignores roads, traffic, one-way streets — actual driving distance is always longer than straight-line distance
quizBank.explanation: Two points 1km apart may require 3km of driving due to roads, barriers, traffic patterns. ETA based on Euclidean distance would be significantly underestimated. Road-network routing (Dijkstra on road graph) gives accurate driving distance and time.

quizBank.question: Why must neighboring geohash cells also be queried when searching for nearby drivers?
options: A driver just outside the primary cell boundary would be missed without neighbor queries
quizBank.answer: A driver just outside the primary cell boundary would be missed without neighbor queries
quizBank.explanation: Geohash cells have sharp boundaries. A rider at cell center and a driver 100m away but in an adjacent cell would not match without neighbor queries. Query the 8 surrounding cells to handle boundary cases. Then filter by actual distance using haversine formula.

quizBank.question: A driver's phone GPS has a 30-second outage. What happens to ongoing trip tracking?
quizBank.answer: GPS outage handling: (1) Last known position retained in Redis for driver. (2) Rider app: interpolate driver position based on last known position + bearing + speed. Show estimated position. (3) After 30 seconds: show 'signal lost' indicator on rider map. (4) Driver app: on GPS recovery, send buffered location updates. Redis updated to current position. (5) Rider map: snap to real position on recovery. (6) Matching: during GPS outage, driver removed from available pool (no location update > 30s = stale). Prevents wrong matching. (7) Trip continues: GPS outage mid-trip doesn't cancel trip. Driver reconnects, continues.

quizBank.question: What Redis data structure is used to store driver locations by geographic cell?
quizBank.answer: Hash (geohash key → driver data)
quizBank.explanation: Redis Hash: HSET drivers:{geohash6} driver_id JSON_payload. HGETALL drivers:u4pruyd returns all drivers in that cell. HDEL removes driver when they leave cell or go offline. Fast O(1) operations. Alternative: Redis GEO commands (GEOADD, GEORADIUS) which use sorted sets internally — more elegant but similar performance.

quizBank.question: How does surge pricing work and how do you ensure it's fair to riders?
quizBank.answer: Surge pricing (dynamic pricing): price = base_fare * surge_multiplier. Surge driven by supply/demand imbalance in geographic area. Supply = available drivers. Demand = ride requests per minute. Low supply or high demand → high multiplier. Fairness: (1) Show multiplier clearly BEFORE confirmation. (2) Require explicit acceptance (Uber's pin entry for high surge). (3) Log surge_multiplier at booking time → fare doesn't increase mid-trip even if surge increases. (4) Cap: maximum multiplier limit (e.g., 8x). (5) Transparency: explain why surge is happening ('High demand due to event nearby'). (6) Cooling off: if rider waits, surge may decrease (alerts when it drops). Legality: some jurisdictions regulate surge pricing during emergencies — must disable.

quizBank.question: The system matches 10 drivers to the same rider request due to a bug. How do you prevent this?
quizBank.answer: Race condition: multiple matching service instances all see the same rider request and simultaneously offer to multiple drivers. Prevention: (1) Distributed lock: before sending offers, acquire Redis lock: SET ride_request:{request_id}:matching NX EX 30. Only one instance gets the lock and proceeds with matching. (2) Idempotent request: request has unique request_id. Matching service records request_id in DB before offering. Other instances check if request already being processed. (3) State machine: ride request starts as 'pending'. Atomic CAS: UPDATE requests SET status='matching' WHERE status='pending' AND id=?. Only succeeds once. (4) Kafka consumer group: single partition for a ride request → single consumer processes it.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a rider requesting a ride in a very dense area (airport) with 100 simultaneous requests?
quizBank.answer: Airport surge scenario: 100 riders, 10 drivers. All 100 request rides simultaneously. Matching service must fairly allocate 10 drivers. Approach: (1) Queue-based matching: queue all requests, process FIFO. First 10 requests get matched. Others notified: 'waiting for driver'. (2) Price-based: highest surge multiplier selection (market clearing). Controversial — effectively prioritizes wealthier riders. (3) Time-ordered + priority: Uber Comfort/Black get priority pool. (4) Driver attraction: surge pricing signal attracts more drivers to airport. (5) Pre-positioning: ML predicts flight arrivals, incentivizes drivers to go to airport before demand spike. (6) Pool rides: reduce individual driver need by combining riders going same direction.

quizBank.question: How do you scale the location service to handle 500,000 driver updates per second?
options: Single Redis server with fast hardware
Redis Cluster partitioned by geohash prefix, with each shard handling a geographic region
Store all locations in a SQL database with geospatial index
quizBank.answer: Redis Cluster partitioned by geohash prefix, with each shard handling a geographic region
quizBank.explanation: 500K updates/sec on single Redis: possible (Redis handles 1M ops/sec) but risky (single point of failure, memory limits). Redis Cluster: partition by geohash prefix (first 2-3 chars). North America on shard 1, Europe on shard 2, etc. Each shard handles ~100K updates/sec. Geographic partitioning: matching queries for one city don't cross shards.

quizBank.question: What communication protocol is used to push driver location updates to the rider app in real time?
quizBank.answer: WebSocket bidirectional connection
quizBank.explanation: WebSocket: persistent bidirectional connection. Server pushes driver location updates to rider without rider polling. Low latency (sub-100ms), low overhead (no HTTP headers per update). Rider app receives new driver lat/lng every 5 seconds (when driver sends update) → updates map marker animation.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and prevent GPS spoofing (a driver faking their location)?
quizBank.answer: GPS spoofing: driver uses app to fake GPS coordinates (e.g., appear at pickup while actually elsewhere). Detection: (1) Speed analysis: if driver's reported location jumps 5km in 1 second → physically impossible → flag. (2) Consistency check: driver's path should follow valid roads (road-snap check). If location is in middle of a river → flag. (3) Trip distance vs reported path: actual distance driven (odometer) vs GPS path length. Significant discrepancy → flag. (4) Device sensor fusion: accelerometer + gyroscope data should correlate with GPS movement. (5) ML model: trained on known spoofer patterns. (6) Action: flag for human review, suspend driver on confirmed spoofing. Consequence: driver charged for actual distance, not spoofed distance.

quizBank.question: How does the app show an accurate ETA for driver arrival that updates in real time?
quizBank.answer: Initial ETA: at match time, calculate route from driver's current location to rider's pickup. OSRM/Mapbox returns duration_seconds. Display 'X minutes away'. Updates: every 30 seconds (or every driver location update), recalculate remaining route from driver's latest GPS position. If driver is stuck in traffic: ETA increases. If driver takes faster route: ETA decreases. Rider app receives ETA updates via WebSocket. Map shows route overlay — rider sees driver position on route. Accuracy: compare predicted vs actual arrival times historically. Use ML to apply correction factor per region/time-of-day. Edge case: driver takes wrong turn → reroute, recalculate ETA. Notification: '2 minutes away' push notification triggers when ETA < 2 minutes.

quizBank.question: A driver rejects a ride offer. What happens next?
options: System waits for driver to reconsider
System offers to next best matching driver, with a 15-second timeout
quizBank.answer: System offers to next best matching driver, with a 15-second timeout
quizBank.explanation: Rejection cascade: (1) Driver rejects → mark driver as 'unavailable' for this request. (2) Next driver in ranked list receives offer. (3) 15-second timeout: if driver doesn't respond, treat as reject, move to next. (4) Track rejection rate: drivers who frequently reject face lower match priority. (5) After exhausting all nearby drivers: expand search radius, retry with new set. (6) If no match in 5 minutes: notify rider, suggest trying again.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'share my trip' safety feature that lets a rider share live location with a contact?
quizBank.answer: Share trip feature: rider generates shareable trip link. Contact opens link → sees real-time map with rider's location and driver info. Architecture: (1) Generate unique share_token (UUID) for trip. (2) Store token → trip_id mapping in Redis with TTL = trip duration + 2 hours. (3) Contact opens link → web page with map. Connects to WebSocket using share_token. (4) Track Service: authenticates share_token, subscribes to rider's trip location stream. (5) Rider location = driver's current location (they're in the same car after pickup). (6) Show: driver name, car info, real-time position on map, ETA to destination. (7) Terminate: link expires when trip completes. Privacy: share_token is opaque, doesn't expose personal info. Rider controls sharing (can revoke).

quizBank.question: How would you design the ML system for proactive driver positioning (predicting where demand will be)?
quizBank.answer: Demand prediction model: forecast ride demand per geographic cell (geohash/H3) per 15-minute time slot. Features: day of week, time of day, weather, local events (concerts, sports), historical demand patterns, holidays. Training: historical trip request data, labeled by origin geohash and time. Model: gradient boosting (LightGBM) or LSTM for time-series patterns. Output: demand heatmap for next 30 minutes. Driver incentives: show predicted surge areas on driver app. 'Head toward downtown — predicted surge in 15 minutes.' Surge pricing pre-announcement: adjust surge earlier when demand predicted. Evaluation: predicted demand vs actual demand per cell. Business impact: reduced wait times, higher driver earnings. Retrain: weekly with new data, deploy via ML pipeline.

quizBank.question: What geospatial precision (geohash length) should be used for finding drivers within 2km?
quizBank.answer: Length 6 (~1.2km x 0.6km)
quizBank.explanation: Geohash length 6 covers ~1.2km x 0.6km area. For 2km radius: query primary cell + 8 neighbors ≈ 3.6km x 1.8km coverage. Then filter by exact haversine distance ≤ 2km. Length 8 is too precise (must query too many cells for 2km). Length 4 is too coarse (returns drivers 20km away — too many to evaluate).

quizBank.question: How do you handle a trip that goes through a tunnel with no GPS signal?
quizBank.answer: Tunnel handling: driver loses GPS signal. (1) Dead reckoning: use last known position + speed + bearing + time elapsed to estimate current position. Accelerometer/gyroscope data helps maintain accuracy. (2) Map matching: match estimated position to known tunnel geometry. Known tunnels in map database — snap position to tunnel path. (3) Rider map: show car moving through tunnel at estimated speed. No jarring jump. (4) On exit: GPS reacquires. Update to actual position. Correct any accumulated dead-reckoning error. (5) Distance calculation: use road distance (from route geometry) not GPS track for fare computation — GPS gaps don't undercount distance. (6) ETA: recalculate on GPS recovery.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between H3 and Geohash for geospatial indexing? When would you use each?
quizBank.answer: Geohash: encodes lat/lng to base32 string. Rectangular grid. Simple, widely supported. Issues: cells are rectangles (different size at different latitudes), neighbor queries slightly complex (8 neighbors, but shape varies). Use for: simple nearby search, most ride-sharing use cases. H3 (Uber's hexagonal grid): divides earth into hexagonal cells. Advantages: (1) Hexagons have equal distance to all 6 neighbors (no diagonal vs edge issue). (2) More uniform cell sizes. (3) Hierarchical: zoom levels 0-15. (4) Better for hexagonal neighbor queries (only 6 neighbors, all equidistant). Use for: demand heatmaps, surge pricing visualization (hexagons look better on maps), advanced spatial analytics. Production: Uber uses H3, Lyft uses S2, most others use Geohash. All work well for basic proximity search.

quizBank.question: How does the system ensure a driver is charged the correct distance even if their GPS has gaps?
quizBank.answer: Use road-snap and interpolation for gaps, compare to road-network distance of planned route
options: Accept whatever GPS distance is reported
Use road-snap and interpolation for gaps, compare to road-network distance of planned route
Ask driver manually at trip end
quizBank.explanation: GPS gap handling: snap recorded GPS points to road network. Interpolate missing sections using road geometry. Compare GPS-computed distance to planned route distance. If GPS shows much shorter than planned: likely GPS gaps. Use planned route distance as floor. Driver incentive to take shortest route maintained. Anti-fraud: if GPS distance >> planned route: flag for review (driver took detour).

quizBank.question: How would you design a pool/shared ride matching system where two riders going the same direction share a car?
quizBank.answer: Pool matching complexity: must find two riders with similar origin AND destination within time and detour constraints. Algorithm: (1) Rider A requests pool → enter pool queue. (2) For rider B's request: find pool requests where route from B's pickup, through A's pickup, to A's destination, to B's destination is feasible (total detour < max_detour_minutes). (3) Calculate: route AB = pickup_B → pickup_A → dropoff_A → dropoff_B. If total_time(AB) < individual_times * threshold: match. (4) Driver assignment: single driver for combined route. (5) ETA shown: includes detour time for each rider. Complexity: matching is computationally expensive. Practical: only try to match within 2-minute window. Use spatial pre-filtering before route computation. Limit: match at most 2 riders per vehicle (Pool, not Bus).

quizBank.question: How do you build the driver rating system to prevent gaming?
quizBank.answer: Rating system: after each trip, rider rates driver (1-5 stars), driver rates rider (1-5 stars). Gaming prevention: (1) Mandatory rating: must rate before booking next trip (reduces skipping). (2) Mutual rating delay: both ratings submitted before either sees the other (prevents retaliation). (3) Threshold for action: driver suspended only if average < 4.0 AND minimum N ratings. (4) Context-aware: very short ratings window (1 day) heavily discounted vs established pattern. (5) Outlier detection: if driver suddenly drops from 4.9 to 2.0: flag for review. Could be unfair ratings. (6) Appeal process: drivers can flag unfair 1-star ratings. (7) Display: show rolling 6-month average, not all-time, to allow recovery. (8) Geographic normalization: some regions consistently rate lower — adjust baseline by city.

quizBank.question: What database is most appropriate for storing driver location history for audit and analytics purposes?
options: Cassandra partitioned by driver_id, clustered by timestamp
quizBank.answer: Cassandra partitioned by driver_id, clustered by timestamp
quizBank.explanation: Location history is time-series, append-only. Access patterns: 'get all locations for driver X during trip Y' (range query by time). Cassandra partition by driver_id: all driver's history on same node. Cluster by recorded_at DESC: range scans by time. High write throughput (100K updates/sec). TTL: auto-delete history older than 1 year. Cassandra excels at exactly this pattern.

quizBank.question: A major event (concert) ends and 50,000 people simultaneously request rides. How does the system handle this?
quizBank.answer: Concert end event: 50,000 ride requests in 60 seconds. System stress: (1) Request queue: API layer queues requests, prevents overload on matching service. (2) Auto-scaling: K8s scales matching service instances based on queue depth. (3) Surge pricing: extreme surge multiplier. Riders see '3x surge'. Many cancel or choose cheaper alternatives. Demand self-regulates. (4) Driver attraction: drivers see high surge zone on app, drive to venue. (5) Staggered release: show 'estimating wait time' while system catches up. (6) Pool optimization: aggressively match pool rides for same destination (all going downtown). (7) Capacity: position extra drivers near venue 15 minutes before event end (demand prediction). (8) Graceful degradation: if queue too long, show estimated wait time rather than failing immediately.

cheatSheet:
# Design a Social Media Platform (Instagram)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- User profiles: bio, profile photo, follower/following counts
- Image/video upload and display
- Feed: posts from followed accounts, ranked
- Stories: 24-hour ephemeral content
- Explore/Discovery: find new content and users
- Hashtags: browse content by tag
- Likes, comments, shares
- Direct Messages (DM)
- Notifications

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability: 99.99%
- Low latency: feed load <200ms, image display <1s
- Scale: 2B MAU, 100M photos/day uploaded, 4.2B likes/day
- Durability: photos and videos stored permanently

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Photo uploads: 100M/day = ~1,160/sec
- Photo size (compressed): avg 500KB → 100M x 500KB = 50TB/day
- Video: assume 10% of uploads are videos, 5MB avg → 10M x 5MB = 50TB/day
- Total storage: 100TB/day = 36.5PB/year (need tiered storage)
- Feed reads: 2B users x 5 feed loads/day = 10B/day = ~116,000/sec
- Likes: 4.2B/day = ~49,000/sec
- Following graph: 2B users x avg 200 follows = 400B edges

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Mobile/Web Client])
    CDN[CDN - CloudFront]
    AG[API Gateway]
    US[Upload Service]
    FS[Feed Service]
    PS[Profile Service]
    StS[Stories Service]
    EX[Explore Service]
    DM[DM Service]
    NS[Notification Service]
    IG[Image Processing Service]
    GS[Graph Service - Follower/Following]
    MediaStore[S3 - Media Storage]
    FCache[Redis - Feed Cache]
    PDB[(Profile DB - PostgreSQL)]
    GDB[(Graph DB - Cassandra)]
    MDB[(Media Metadata - Cassandra)]
    Kafka[Event Bus]

    Client --> CDN
    CDN --> AG
    AG --> US
    AG --> FS
    AG --> PS
    AG --> StS
    AG --> EX
    AG --> DM
    US --> IG
    IG --> MediaStore
    IG --> MDB
    IG --> Kafka
    Kafka --> FS
    Kafka --> NS
    FS --> FCache
    FS --> GS
    GS --> GDB
    MediaStore --> CDN
```

## 4. Image Upload & Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant US as Upload Service
    participant S3 as Object Store
    participant IP as Image Processor
    participant CDN
    participant Kafka

    U->>US: POST /upload (multipart or presigned URL request)
    US-->>U: Presigned S3 URL + media_id
    U->>S3: PUT image directly (bypasses servers)
    S3->>IP: S3 trigger event
    IP->>IP: Resize to multiple sizes (thumb, medium, large)
    IP->>IP: Apply filters if requested
    IP->>IP: Generate perceptual hash (for dedup / moderation)
    IP->>S3: Store all variants
    IP->>Kafka: media_ready event
    Kafka->>US: Update post status: published
    US->>CDN: Warm CDN for popular content
    US-->>U: Post published notification
```

### Image Variants Generated
```
Original:    Stored as-is (for download)
Large:       1080px width (feed display)
Medium:      640px width (profile grid)
Thumbnail:   150x150px (search results, comments)
Story:       Optimized for 9:16 aspect ratio
```

## 5. Follow Graph

### Data Model (Cassandra)
```sql
-- Who does user X follow?
CREATE TABLE following (
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id)
);

-- Who follows user X? (for fan-out)
CREATE TABLE followers (
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (followee_id, follower_id)
);
```

### Scaling the Graph
- 400B edges: Cassandra handles with partition by user_id
- Celebrity problem: Selena Gomez has 400M followers
- Fan-out on write: would create 400M feed updates per post
- Solution: hybrid fan-out (same as news feed problem)

## 6. Feed Generation

### Hybrid Fan-out Strategy
- Normal users (<100K followers): fan-out on write
- Celebrity (>100K followers): fan-out on read
- Inactive users (>30 days): no fan-out, compute on next login

### Feed Ranking ML Pipeline
```
Signals:
- Affinity: how much does this user interact with poster?
- Recency: newer posts scored higher
- Content type: video > carousel > single image
- Engagement velocity: fast-growing likes boost visibility
- User interests: topics user engages with

Pipeline:
1. Candidate generation: 1000 posts from feed cache
2. ML ranking: score each candidate
3. Diversity filter: max 3 consecutive posts from same user
4. Ad insertion: every 5th post
5. Return top 20 posts
```

## 7. Stories Architecture

### Ephemeral Design
- Stories expire after 24 hours
- Separate from main feed (different UX)
- Viewed story tracked per user
- Story ring: avatars of friends who posted stories

### Data Model
```sql
CREATE TABLE stories (
    story_id    UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id     BIGINT,
    media_url   TEXT,
    story_type  TEXT,  -- image, video, boomerang
    duration_ms INT,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP,
    expires_at  TIMESTAMP,  -- created_at + 24 hours
    view_count  INT DEFAULT 0
) WITH default_time_to_live = 86400;  -- Cassandra TTL: 24 hours

CREATE TABLE story_views (
    story_id   UUID,
    viewer_id  BIGINT,
    viewed_at  TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (story_id, viewer_id)
) WITH default_time_to_live = 86400;
```

### Story Ring Generation
- Fetch: who does user follow? (from followers table)
- For each followee: any active stories (created_at > now - 24h)?
- Sorted by: unviewed first, then by recency
- Cache: store story ring in Redis with 1-hour TTL

## 8. Explore / Discovery

### How Instagram Explore Works
- Collaborative filtering: users with similar tastes like same posts
- Identify 'seeds' (posts user recently engaged with)
- Find other users who also engaged with seeds → see what else they like
- Content-based: posts with similar visual features (ML embedding)
- Trending: posts with fast engagement velocity in user's interest graph
- Location-based: posts near user's location

### Hashtag System
```sql
CREATE TABLE hashtag_posts (
    hashtag    TEXT,
    post_id    BIGINT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (hashtag, created_at, post_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC);

-- Query: latest posts with #sunset
SELECT post_id FROM hashtag_posts
WHERE hashtag = 'sunset'
AND created_at > (now - 7 days)
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50;
```

## 9. API Design

```
Media:
POST /api/v1/media/upload/initiate   — get presigned URL + media_id
POST /api/v1/posts                   — create post with media_ids
GET  /api/v1/posts/{post_id}         — post details
DEL  /api/v1/posts/{post_id}         — delete post

Feed:
GET  /api/v1/feed?cursor={post_id}&limit=20  — paginated feed
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/posts        — user's profile grid
GET  /api/v1/explore                 — explore feed
GET  /api/v1/hashtags/{tag}          — hashtag feed

Social:
POST /api/v1/posts/{id}/like         — like/unlike
POST /api/v1/users/{id}/follow       — follow/unfollow
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/followers    — follower list
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/following    — following list

Stories:
POST /api/v1/stories                 — create story
GET  /api/v1/stories/feed            — story ring
POST /api/v1/stories/{id}/view       — mark as viewed
```

## 10. Like Counter at Scale

- 49,000 likes/sec: too many to write directly to post DB
- Redis INCR: like:{post_id} → atomic in-memory counter
- Batch write to DB every 5 seconds
- Display: read from Redis (real-time count)
- Idempotency: maintain SET liked_by:{post_id} per user → prevent double-like
- Problem: liked_by set for viral post (10M likes) → 10M entries in Redis set
- Solution: store likes in Cassandra, check with Bloom filter for quick 'did I like this?' check

## 11. Media Storage & CDN

- Object store: S3 (origin)
- CDN: Akamai/CloudFront (edge delivery)
- Image URL structure: `cdn.instagram.com/{user_id}/{media_id}/{size}.jpg`
- Cache TTL: images immutable → 1 year
- Stories: shorter TTL (24h) since they expire
- Private accounts: signed URLs with expiry
- Deduplication: perceptual hash (pHash) → detect near-identical uploads

## 12. Monitoring
- Feed load latency p99 (target <200ms)
- Image upload success rate
- Story TTL cleanup job lag
- Fan-out queue depth (Kafka lag)
- CDN hit rate for media
- Like/comment event processing latency
- Explore recommendation CTR

resources.title: System Design Interview Vol 1 - Alex Xu

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows Instagram as photo sharing app. Can describe upload → share → view flow.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Designs upload pipeline with S3. Knows about CDN for image delivery. Can describe follow graph.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs image processing pipeline, hybrid fan-out feed, Redis like counters, stories with TTL, hashtag system.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Implements explore/discovery system, ML feed ranking, story ring with view tracking, DM system, content moderation.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs multi-region photo storage, visual search (search by photo), reels recommendation, creator monetization, and GDPR data handling.

plan.overview: 10-session plan covering media upload, follow graph, feed generation, stories, and explore.

sessions.title: Requirements & Scale
activities: Identify hardest problems: media storage, feed generation, graph scale.

sessions.title: Feed Generation
activities: Redis sorted set for pre-built feed.

sessions.title: Content Moderation
activities: Perceptual hash for known bad content.
ML classifier for new content (NSFW, violence).

sessions.title: Direct Messages & Notifications
activities: DM: similar to chat system design.
Notification triggers: like, comment, follow, DM.

sessions.title: Mock Interview
activities: Must cover: upload pipeline, feed (fan-out), stories, likes at scale.

quizBank.question: Why are multiple image size variants (thumbnail, medium, large) generated for each uploaded photo?
options: Different screen sizes and contexts need different resolutions to save bandwidth and improve performance
quizBank.answer: Different screen sizes and contexts need different resolutions to save bandwidth and improve performance
quizBank.explanation: Profile grid: 150px thumbnails. Feed: 640px medium. Full view: 1080px. Sending 1080px everywhere wastes bandwidth on profile grid (3 images visible = 3x1080px = wasteful). Sending 150px everywhere: blurry in full view. Optimizing by context: reduces page load by 5-10x.

quizBank.question: How does Instagram handle the 'celebrity problem' where Selena Gomez (400M followers) posts a photo?
options: Fan-out on write to all 400M followers
Store post once; celebrities' posts fetched on read; normal users get fan-out on write
Delay celebrity posts until off-peak hours
quizBank.answer: Store post once; celebrities' posts fetched on read; normal users get fan-out on write
quizBank.explanation: Hybrid fan-out: celebrities (>100K followers) use fan-out on read. When user opens feed: normal followees' posts come from pre-built feed cache. Celebrity's posts fetched from their post timeline and merged. This avoids 400M fan-out operations per celebrity post.

quizBank.question: How are Instagram Stories automatically deleted after 24 hours?
options: Background job scans and deletes expired stories every minute
Cassandra default_time_to_live (TTL) automatically expires rows after 24 hours
Stories are moved to archive after 24 hours
quizBank.answer: Cassandra default_time_to_live (TTL) automatically expires rows after 24 hours
quizBank.explanation: Cassandra TTL: set default_time_to_live = 86400 (seconds) on stories table. Rows automatically deleted after 24 hours with no application logic needed. Efficient: Cassandra handles TTL cleanup during compaction. CDN cache for story media: set max-age=86400 so cached media also expires naturally.

quizBank.question: A photo uploaded by a user is found to violate content policy 1 hour after posting. How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Immediate response: (1) Soft delete: mark post as is_deleted=true in post DB. Feed service filters deleted posts. (2) CDN purge: proactively purge CDN cache for all image variants (expensive but necessary for policy violations). (3) Object store: media files remain (for audit/legal) but access blocked via signed URL invalidation or storage policy. (4) User notification: inform user of policy violation, link to policy. (5) Appeal: user can appeal within 30 days. (6) Hash blacklist: add perceptual hash of image to blocklist — prevent re-upload. (7) Account action: first offense warning, repeated violations → suspension.

quizBank.question: How do you implement the 'story ring' — showing circles of friends who have active stories?
quizBank.answer: Story ring generation: (1) Fetch user's following list from graph DB (Cassandra following table). (2) For each followee: check if they have active stories (created_at > now - 24h). Query: SELECT story_id FROM stories WHERE user_id=? AND expires_at > now(). (3) Sorting: unviewed stories first (check story_views table for viewer_id=current_user). Then by most recent story. (4) Avatars: profile photo from user profile cache. (5) Caching: store story ring result in Redis per user. TTL: 5 minutes (acceptable staleness). On follow/unfollow: invalidate cache. (6) Real-time: when someone posts a new story, push notification to followers → invalidate story ring cache.

quizBank.question: How is the like count for a photo stored and retrieved at 49,000 likes/sec?
options: Directly in SQL posts table, updated per like
Redis INCR for fast atomic increment, batch-written to SQL DB every 5 seconds
Counted by querying likes table at read time
Stored in Elasticsearch for fast aggregation
quizBank.answer: Redis INCR for fast atomic increment, batch-written to SQL DB every 5 seconds
quizBank.explanation: 49K likes/sec directly to MySQL: overloads single row. Redis INCR like:{post_id}: atomic, in-memory, handles 100K+ ops/sec. Background job reads Redis counters every 5 seconds, batch-writes to MySQL. Display: read from Redis (live count). DB is eventual source of truth (5s lag acceptable). Viral post: only Redis counter hot, not DB.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and prevent upload of child sexual abuse material (CSAM)?
quizBank.answer: CSAM detection is both legal requirement and ethical imperative. Multi-layer: (1) PhotoDNA (Microsoft): perceptual hash matching against known CSAM database (maintained by NCMEC). Every uploaded image hashed and compared. Match = immediate block, no human review needed. (2) ML classifier: detect new/unknown CSAM that isn't in hash database. Computer vision model trained to detect content. (3) User reports: report mechanism → immediate human review queue. Content reviewed within hours. (4) NCMEC reporting: legally required to report confirmed CSAM to NCMEC within 24 hours. Provide user data to law enforcement. (5) Account suspension: immediate permanent ban for any confirmed CSAM upload. (6) Data retention: preserve evidence for law enforcement. Never retain CSAM on servers — hash only.

quizBank.question: How would you design the 'close friends' feature where stories are shared with a select group?
quizBank.answer: Close friends list: user maintains a close_friends list (subset of following). Stories marked as close_friends_only=true. Access control: (1) Story ring: only show close-friends-only story ring to users in close_friends list. (2) Story viewer list: only close friends can view the story. (3) Backend: when fetching story ring: check if current user is in story poster's close friends list. SELECT 1 FROM close_friends WHERE user_id=poster AND friend_id=viewer. (4) Data model: close_friends table (user_id, friend_id). Partitioned by user_id. (5) Privacy: story URLs must be signed — non-close-friends can't access even with URL. (6) Notifications: close friends story has green ring indicator (different from regular story).

quizBank.question: How do you store the follower graph for 2 billion users with an average of 200 follows each?
options: Two Cassandra tables: following (follower_id → followees) and followers (followee_id → followers)
Redis sorted sets for all edges
quizBank.answer: Two Cassandra tables: following (follower_id → followees) and followers (followee_id → followers)
quizBank.explanation: 400B edges total. Two complementary tables: (1) following: partition by follower_id → 'who does user X follow?' for feed generation. (2) followers: partition by followee_id → 'who follows user Y?' for fan-out notifications. Denormalized for query efficiency — each follow creates 2 rows. Cassandra handles 400B rows across nodes. Alternative: MySQL is too slow for full table scans at this scale.

quizBank.question: How would you design the Instagram Explore page recommendation system?
quizBank.answer: Multi-signal collaborative filtering: (1) Seed selection: pick user's recent high-engagement posts (liked, saved, commented). (2) User-user similarity: find users who engaged with same seeds. These are 'taste twins'. (3) Candidate generation: what else do taste twins engage with? Those posts are candidates. (4) Content-based: ML visual embeddings of posts user liked → find visually similar posts (CLIP model). (5) Ranking: ML model scores candidates. Features: affinity to author, content quality (engagement rate), diversity (topic mix), freshness. (6) Filtering: remove already-seen posts, blocked users, low-quality content. (7) Diversity: max 3 posts from same creator. (8) Infrastructure: offline training (Spark), daily candidate generation, online ranking per request. (9) Personalization: separate explore tab per user, computed from their unique engagement history.

quizBank.question: What is a perceptual hash (pHash) used for in image upload processing?
options: To encrypt the image for storage
To detect near-duplicate or visually similar images (same image re-uploaded or slightly modified)
To compress the image for CDN delivery
To categorize image by color palette
quizBank.answer: To detect near-duplicate or visually similar images (same image re-uploaded or slightly modified)
quizBank.explanation: pHash: generates a 64-bit fingerprint based on image visual content. Similar images produce similar hashes (small Hamming distance). Use cases: (1) Deduplication: don't store same image twice. (2) Copyright detection: artist's image reposted. (3) CSAM detection: PhotoDNA uses pHash to match known illegal content. (4) Content moderation: blocklisted images can be detected even after cropping or filter application.

quizBank.question: A user with 10M followers posts a photo. How does it appear in followers' feeds?
quizBank.answer: Hybrid fan-out: 10M followers exceeds normal user threshold (say 100K). Handle as celebrity. Post creation: (1) Write post to post DB (single write). (2) Publish post_created event to Kafka. (3) Fan-out worker: for celebrity posts, skip fan-out to followers' feed caches. (4) Feed service: when follower requests feed — fetch their pre-built feed cache (from normal followees) + celebrity posts (fetched on-demand from post DB). (5) Merge and rank: celebrity's posts merged with feed cache, ML ranking applied. (6) Caching celebrity's post: celebrity's timeline cached in Redis (recent 500 posts) → read is still fast. (7) Result: follower sees celebrity's photo within a few seconds of posting.

quizBank.question: Why does Instagram use Cassandra for storing media metadata instead of MySQL?
quizBank.answer: Media metadata (post table) access patterns: insert on upload (high write volume: 1,160/sec), lookup by post_id (key-value), lookup by user_id (user's profile grid). Scale: 100M posts/day, years of history = billions of rows. Cassandra advantages: (1) Linear horizontal scalability — add nodes to handle more throughput. (2) High write throughput — LSM-tree storage optimized for writes. (3) Key-value access pattern matches Cassandra's partition key model. (4) No complex joins needed — post data is self-contained. MySQL would require: careful sharding, complex cluster management, limited write throughput per shard. Cassandra limitations: no ad-hoc queries, no ACID across rows, schema must match access patterns.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'suggested users to follow' based on social graph connections?
quizBank.answer: Friend-of-friend (FoF) algorithm: (1) Get user's current following list (set A). (2) For each user in A: get their following list. (3) Collect all users that A's followees follow (set B). Remove users already in A. Remove current user. (4) Count: how many of current user's followees follow each candidate in B. (5) Higher count = stronger suggestion (mutual connections). (6) Filter: remove blocked users, private accounts (without follow request). (7) Rank: FoF count + account type + content interest alignment. (8) Cache: compute suggestions daily, cache in Redis. Refresh on follow/unfollow. (9) Alternative signals: common hashtag engagement, similar profile (bio analysis), location-based (shared city). (10) ML enhancement: train model on which suggestions users actually follow → predict follow probability.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of cursor-based pagination in Instagram's feed vs offset pagination?
options: Cursor pagination is faster to implement
Cursor pagination is stable when new posts are added at top; offset can cause duplicates or missing posts
Cursor pagination requires less database storage
quizBank.answer: Cursor pagination is stable when new posts are added at top; offset can cause duplicates or missing posts
quizBank.explanation: Offset: LIMIT 20 OFFSET 20 → if new post inserted: page 2 includes last item of page 1 (duplicate) or skips first of old page 2. Cursor: anchor on last_seen_post_id → return posts where post_id < cursor. Stable regardless of new posts. Instagram feed: cursor = lowest post_id in current page. Next page: load more before that ID.

quizBank.question: How do you handle video upload and playback at scale (Reels)?
quizBank.answer: Video pipeline differs from photos: (1) Size: videos up to 90 seconds = ~50MB. Multipart upload to S3 (10MB chunks). (2) Transcoding: similar to YouTube — FFmpeg workers transcode to H.264 MP4. Multiple resolutions: 1080p, 720p, 480p. Multiple frame rates. (3) HLS output: segment into 2-second chunks for adaptive bitrate. (4) Thumbnail: extract cover frame. Allow user to choose. (5) CDN: video segments served from CDN. Long TTL (immutable). (6) Autoplay: load first 5 seconds while scrolling for smooth autoplay. (7) Audio: mute by default, user unmutes. (8) Transcription: captions generated via ASR for accessibility. (9) Recommendation: separate Reels recommendation model — optimize for watch-through rate, rewatches. (10) Analytics: track per-second viewer retention.

quizBank.question: How would you design the 'tagged in a photo' feature and make it searchable?
quizBank.answer: Tag creation: when user creates post, they can tag other users (search by username → select user_id). Tags stored in photo_tags table (post_id, tagged_user_id, x_position, y_position, created_at). Notification: tagged user gets notified immediately via notification service. Privacy: (1) Tagged user can remove themselves from tag (delete from photo_tags). (2) Private accounts: can restrict who can tag them (all, following only, nobody). (3) Approval required: option to review tags before they appear on profile. Searchability: (1) Reverse index: tagged_user_id → [post_ids]. Query for user's tagged photos. (2) Cache: list of tag_post_ids per user in Redis (recently tagged, up to 500). (3) Profile page: 'Tagged' tab shows all approved tags. (4) Index: Elasticsearch for full tag graph search.

quizBank.question: How does Instagram ensure images load quickly for users in slow network regions?
options: Send full resolution images with compression
Use CDN edge nodes globally + serve smallest usable resolution + progressive JPEG loading
Store images near each user's home region
Pre-download all feed images when on WiFi
quizBank.answer: Use CDN edge nodes globally + serve smallest usable resolution + progressive JPEG loading
quizBank.explanation: Multi-strategy: (1) CDN edge (200+ locations): serve from nearest POP, <10ms network latency. (2) Adaptive quality: detect connection speed, serve 480px instead of 1080px on slow connection. (3) Progressive JPEG: displays low-quality preview immediately, progressively sharpens as bytes arrive. (4) WebP format: 25-35% smaller than JPEG at same quality. (5) Lazy loading: images below viewport load only when scrolling approaches. (6) Pre-fetch: next 2-3 images in feed preloaded while reading current.

quizBank.question: A user deletes their Instagram account. What needs to happen to their data?
quizBank.answer: Account deletion pipeline: (1) Immediate: mark account as deleted/inactive. Remove from search results. Other users can't find them. (2) Content removal: posts, stories, comments → soft delete (hide from feed, mark is_deleted=true). (3) Follow relationships: remove from all followers' following lists, remove all their followers' graph entries. Kafka fan-out to update affected users' feed caches. (4) DMs: delete their messages (GDPR right to erasure). Recipient still has thread but sender's messages replaced with 'deleted message'. (5) Media files: S3 deletion scheduled (can take 30 days per GDPR). CDN purge. (6) Likes/comments on others' posts: anonymize or remove. (7) Data export: before deletion, provide data export (GDPR right to data portability). (8) Reactivation window: 30-day recovery period before permanent deletion. (9) Audit log: retain minimal log for legal compliance.

quizBank.question: How would you design a visual search feature ('search by image')?
quizBank.answer: Visual search: user uploads photo or long-presses any image in feed → find visually similar posts. Architecture: (1) Image embeddings: ML model (CLIP, EfficientNet) converts each image to 512-dim vector embedding. Computed at upload time, stored in vector DB. (2) Vector DB: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) or Pinecone — optimized for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. (3) Query: user's image → embedding → ANN query in vector DB → top-K similar embeddings → post IDs. (4) Ranking: re-rank by combined score: visual similarity + engagement quality. (5) Scale: 100M posts/day × 512 floats = 200GB/day of embeddings. Use quantization (INT8) to reduce by 4x. (6) Index: FAISS IVF index for billion-scale ANN. Trade-off: approximate (may miss some similar images) vs exact (too slow). (7) Pipeline: upload → resize → embedding model → vector DB write (async).

quizBank.question: What technique prevents the same user from liking a photo multiple times?
options: Redis SET membership check before incrementing counter
Both A and B are valid approaches
quizBank.answer: Both A and B are valid approaches
quizBank.explanation: Two-phase approach: (1) Redis SET liked_by:{post_id}: SADD before INCR. SADD returns 0 if already member → skip increment. Fast, in-memory. (2) DB unique constraint: INSERT INTO likes (user_id, post_id) → unique constraint violation if duplicate. Two layers: Redis for speed, DB for truth. For large posts (10M likes): Redis set too large. Alternative: Cassandra likes table with (user_id, post_id) as PK → idempotent INSERT.

quizBank.question: How would you design the hashtag trending system for Instagram?
quizBank.answer: Trending hashtag pipeline: (1) Event collection: every post creation with hashtags → Kafka post_created event containing hashtag list. (2) Real-time aggregation: Kafka Streams or Flink: sliding window count per hashtag (last 60 minutes). (3) Baseline comparison: compare current count to same window last week. Trending_score = current_count / historical_baseline. (4) Velocity filter: require minimum absolute count (e.g., 1000 posts/hour) to trend. (5) Geographic trending: separate trending per country/city. Geo-tag of posts used. (6) Storage: trending_hashtags table in Redis sorted set: ZADD trending:{country} score hashtag. Top 30 per country. (7) Update frequency: recalculate every 5 minutes. (8) Display: Explore page 'Trending' section, hashtag search result header. (9) Manipulation prevention: rate limit new accounts' hashtag posts. Detect coordinated hashtag campaigns.
